85ca6d52-81f0-42e6-944e-25f99b042170.titulo: What a Light Designer / Light Operator Does
85ca6d52-81f0-42e6-944e-25f99b042170.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Before you touch any console, understand who you want to be: the professional who turns watts into emotion.

Stage lighting is not just 'turning on lights.' It is a visual language that tells stories, marks the rhythm of the music, and directs the audience's gaze. In this lesson you will map the roles, responsibilities, and chain of command of a production, distinguishing roles many people confuse: the Light Designer (LD), who conceives the design; the operator, who runs the console; and the lighting technician/roadie, who rigs, connects, and maintains the rig. We will also situate where light fits among sound, video, stage, and direction.

  • Lighting as a language: To light is to communicate. Color temperature, intensity, distribution, and light pattern are the lighting designer's 'words.' The same stage with warm, low light conveys intimacy; with cold, intense, moving light it conveys party energy. Mastering this grammar is what separates the operator from the designer.
  • Light Designer (LD): The author of the visual concept. Defines the color palette, angles, key moments, and how the light follows the narrative or the setlist. Produces the technical rider, the plot (lighting plan), and the cue list. On big tours the LD often operates the console; on smaller productions, they hand off the programming to a local operator.
  • Light operator: Runs the show at the console. Can run a pre-programmed cuelist (hitting GO on each song) or play 'on the fly' (busking), reacting to the band in real time. Must know the console's navigation deeply, have musical instinct, and stay calm under pressure — a wrong GO at the wrong moment ruins the climax.
  • Lighting technician / roadie: Responsible for the physical side: rigging truss, hanging and addressing fixtures, running DMX and AC cables, focusing fixtures, and doing maintenance. They ensure that when the operator presses a button, something actually happens. In small productions, one person combines LD, operator, and technician.
  • The chain of command: In a typical production: Direction/Artist sets the intention → Producer makes it viable → LD designs → lighting crew rigs → operator executes. Light constantly dialogues with the sound operator (PA), the stage manager, and video/LED. Clear intercom communication is part of the job.
  • Where you work: Concerts and festivals, theater, TV and film, corporate events, weddings, churches, clubs, fashion shows, and art installations. Each niche has its own pace and demands: theater values precision and cue repeatability; a festival show values impact and busking; corporate values branding and clean light on the speaker.

In practice: At a mid-size rock band show, the LD arrives with the rider already sent to the venue. The venue delivers the rig already built. The LD/operator does the 'patch,' matching addresses with what is hung, programs colors and positions during soundcheck, and at night operates the console reading the band: pulling strobes on the chorus, closing everything (blackout) at the end of a ballad, and opening a warm amber wash on the next song. Three roles, sometimes just one person.

Summary

  • Lighting is about telling stories with light, not just switching on fixtures.
  • The LD conceives, the operator executes, the technician rigs and maintains — in small productions all of this can be one person.
  • The lighting professional works as a team and in constant dialogue with sound, stage, and direction.

LD, operator, and crew: who does what

The Light Designer (LD) is the one who creates the show: defines the concept, the colors, and the mood, draws the plot (lighting map), picks the fixtures, and programs the scenes and cues. The operator is the one who runs the show live — operates the console, busks, and reacts to the music. Around them is the crew: rigger (hoisting and structure), fitter/electrician (hangs, cables, and powers), and roadie/production (logistics). On small crews one person combines roles, but knowing the division organizes the work — and shows the career path: you start rigging and operating, then grow into programming and designing.

Test it in the interactive below: match each task to its role.

2841b269-4af1-4045-a5e0-5aa4242eb4ae.titulo: Physics of Light: color, intensity, distance, and angle
2841b269-4af1-4045-a5e0-5aa4242eb4ae.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Light obeys physical laws. Those who ignore them fight the light; those who master them sculpt with it.

This is the most 'scientific' lesson of the module, and the foundation of everything that follows. You will understand color temperature (Kelvin), what CRI is, how intensity falls off with distance (the famous inverse-square law), why the angle of light completely changes the reading of a face or object, and how shadow and contrast create depth. These are concepts that appear in every patch and focus decision.

  • Color temperature (Kelvin): Measures the tone of white light. Low values (≈2700–3200 K) are warm/yellowish, reminiscent of an incandescent lamp and create coziness. High values (≈5600–6500 K) are cool/bluish, reminiscent of daylight, conveying energy or coldness. RGBW LED fixtures or variable-white fixtures let you adjust this. Mixing temperatures on stage is an aesthetic choice, not a mistake.
  • CRI (color rendering): The Color Rendering Index (0 to 100) indicates how faithfully the light shows the real colors of skin, costume, and set. High CRI (>90) is essential for TV, film, and fashion; cheap LEDs with low CRI make skin look 'greenish' or washed out on camera. Always check the CRI on the datasheet when there is a camera at the event.
  • Intensity and the inverse-square law: Perceived intensity falls off with the square of distance: doubling the distance between fixture and object reduces the illuminance to about 1/4. That is why a fixture that lights well at 5 m looks weak at 10 m. In projects, this requires balancing source power against desired coverage and positioning fixtures with the real distance to the target in mind.
  • Angle of incidence: Where the light comes from changes everything. Front light flattens and reveals detail (good for readability, bad for volume); side light (45° is classic) models and adds relief; backlight (behind) rims the silhouette and separates the artist from the background; light from below (uplight) creates tension/horror; light from above closes the eyes too much. The LD combines angles to sculpt the body in space.
  • Distance, focus, and shadow quality: Close sources produce hard shadows and strong contrast; distant or large/diffuse sources produce soft shadows. Small, hard sources (beam) cut through the air; broad sources (wash, footlight) bathe. The apparent size of the source, not just distance, defines the 'hardness' of the shadow.
  • Contrast, depth, and density: Depth is born from the interplay of light and shadow. Lighting everything evenly leaves the scene 'flat.' Varying the density (intensity) between planes — more light on the artist, less on the background — creates layers and three-dimensionality. Controlled contrast guides the eye without dazzling the rest of the scene.

Equipment / details

  • Color meter / Kelvin reference: Not a piece of equipment you buy for the rig, but a mental reference. Knowing that 3200 K is the classic theatrical warm white and 5600 K is daylight white helps you choose correction gels (CTB/CTO) and set up the white of LEDs.
  • Variable-white fixtures (tunable white): PARs and LED panels that adjust the Kelvin from ~2700 to ~6500 K via DMX. Useful in TV, corporate, and theater when you want to change the 'mood' of the white without swapping gels.

Step by step

  1. Test the inverse-square law: Point a PAR at a wall 2 m away and observe the size and brightness of the circle. Move it to 4 m: the circle doubles in diameter and the brightness drops to about 1/4. This trains your intuition for how much power you need at each distance.
  2. Feel the angle: Ask someone to stand. Light from the front, then from 45° to the side, then from behind, then from below. Observe how each angle changes the reading of the face and body. This exercise is the basis of focus in any production.
  3. Compare shadow hardness: Compare the shadow of a beam (small/hard source) with that of a diffuse wash on the same wall. The difference in the sharpness of the shadow's edge is the 'quality' of the light.

In practice: At a corporate TV event, the speaker looked 'sick' on camera. The technician measured: the LED PARs had CRI ~80 and inconsistent temperature. Swapping them for CRI >90 fixtures at 4000 K and balancing the front intensity with a slight backlight, the skin regained natural color and the speaker gained volume and separation from the background.

Summary

  • Kelvin defines the tone of white; CRI defines the fidelity of colors.
  • Intensity falls off with the square of distance — distance and power go together.
  • Angle and shadow quality sculpt volume and depth in the scene.

Intensity, distance, angle, and color

Four quantities rule light. The inverse-square law: illuminance (lux) falls off with the square of distance — move the fixture to twice the distance and the light on the target becomes 1/4. The beam angle defines the size of the pool: a wider angle spreads the same flux (lumens) over a larger area, so each point receives less lux — a big, weak pool, or a tight, strong one. Color temperature (Kelvin) ranges from warm (~3200K, tungsten, orange) to cool (~5600–6500K, daylight, bluish). Measures: flux in lumens, intensity in candelas, illuminance on the target in lux.

See it all relate in the simulator below.

85926bc6-de4b-421c-aff5-e263a9a9e72b.titulo: Documentation, standards (NRs), and electrical and height safety
85926bc6-de4b-421c-aff5-e263a9a9e72b.conteudo_rico:
To begin: A good show is one that ends with everyone in one piece. Safety is not bureaucracy — it is the first technical skill.

Stage lighting involves power electricity, suspended structures, and work at height. This lesson covers the professional documents to work in Brazil, the relevant Regulatory Standards (NRs), and the fundamentals of electrical and rigging safety that every technician must internalize before climbing a truss or connecting an AC cable.

  • Professional documentation: To work legally in Brazil: CPF, and, if self-employed, proper registration with the tax authority. Electrical engineering roles may require registration with CREA. Work on elevated structures requires NR 35 training (work at height). RF equipment (wireless microphones) may require ANATEL authorization — relevant when light and sound share the same production.
  • Essential NRs for lighting: NR 6 (PPE — helmet, gloves, safety harness, footwear); NR 10 (safety in electrical installations and services — fundamental for anyone working with AC); NR 35 (work at height — mandatory for truss and grid assembly); NR 12 (machinery), NR 23 (fire), and NR 17 (ergonomics) also come up. The source material also cites NR 7, NR 9, NR 15, and NR 33 (confined spaces).
  • Electrical safety — the basics that save lives: Knowing the voltage (110/220 V), current (amperes), and power (watts) of each fixture is mandatory for sizing circuits. Add up the watts of the rig, calculate the current (P = V × I), and never overload a phase. Use proper cabling and plugs, grounding, and RCDs/breakers. An undersized AC cable heats up and catches fire.
  • Rigging and work at height: Every hung fixture needs a primary attachment (clamp) and a secondary one (safety steel cable / safety). Truss and hoists have a working load limit (WLL) that must never be exceeded. Anyone climbing uses a helmet, harness, and lifeline (NR 35). No one walks under a suspended load.
  • PPE and collective protection in daily work: Personal Protective Equipment: helmet, gloves, goggles, safety footwear, harness/lanyard for height. Collective Protective Equipment: area signage, isolation under the truss, grounding. Fixtures and dimmers get hot — gloves when handling after use prevent burns.
  • Event documents: Besides the rider, events require an operating permit, a technical safety report, Fire Department authorization, and, for electrical installations, an ART (Technical Responsibility Note) signed by an electrical engineer. Without these papers, the show can be shut down.

Equipment / details

  • Lighting PPE (helmet, gloves, NR 35 harness): Helmet for grid assembly; gloves to handle hot fixtures and cables; safety harness with a double lanyard and energy absorber for any work above 2 m. Closed footwear always.
  • Safety steel cable (safety/wire): Mandatory secondary attachment on every suspended fixture. If the primary clamp fails, the safety holds the fixture and prevents it from falling onto the audience. It must have a rating compatible with the fixture's weight.
  • Multimeter / clamp ammeter: Measures voltage, continuity, and current. Used to check live/neutral, verify grounding, and measure the real consumption of a circuit before overloading it.

Step by step

  1. Calculate the load of a circuit: Add up the power (W) of all fixtures on the same circuit. Divide by the voltage (110 or 220 V) to find the current in amperes (I = P ÷ V). Make sure this current stays below the breaker's and cable's limit, with a safety margin (do not exceed ~80% of the rating).
  2. Hang a fixture safely: 1) Attach the clamp to the truss and tighten. 2) Run the safety steel cable around the truss and attach it to the fixture. 3) Check the tilt lock. 4) Connect AC and DMX leaving a cable loop (no tension on the connector). 5) Confirm nothing is under the load.
  3. Power-up checklist: Before turning on: check grounding, absence of stripped cables, correct plugs, and that the breaker supports the load. Turn on in stages (not all at once) to avoid a current spike.

In practice: During a festival build, the technician put 12 moving heads of 600 W each on a single 220 V phase. Quick math: 12 × 600 = 7,200 W ÷ 220 V ≈ 33 A — above the 32 A breaker and close to the cable's limit. He redistributed half the fixtures to another phase, avoiding a trip in the middle of the show and overheating of the AC cable.

Summary

  • Electrical and rigging safety comes before any visual effect.
  • NR 10 (electricity) and NR 35 (height) are the most critical standards for lighting.
  • Every suspended fixture needs a primary clamp and a safety steel cable.

Pre-show inspection: NRs and safety

Before any show, the operator does a safety inspection. On the electrical (NR-10) side: grounding, RCD (residual-current device) in the panel, load within the breaker/generator (no overload), and protected cables. On work at height and rigging (NR-35): harness/lanyard with anchoring, safety cable (secondary) on each fixture, isolated area beneath the rig, and motors/slings inspected within the WLL. Add the documentation (rider, ART/report when applicable) and the crew's PPE. Some items are critical: without them, the show does not go up — electrical and height safety are non-negotiable. Documenting the inspection protects people and you.

Run the inspection in the simulator below and try to clear the show.

8377dd1c-cdfe-40ae-9ae4-87686d58b7b1.titulo: What DMX512 is and how the signal travels
8377dd1c-cdfe-40ae-9ae4-87686d58b7b1.conteudo_rico:
To begin: DMX is the language the console speaks to the lights. If it fails, nothing lights up the way you want.

Everything you program at the console only reaches the fixtures because a protocol exists: DMX512. This lesson explains what a universe is, what channels and values (0–255) are, how the signal travels over XLR cables, why a terminator is used, and how DMX connects to (and is sometimes replaced by) networks like Art-Net and sACN. It is the technical foundation of the addressing you will do in the next lesson.

  • The DMX512 protocol: DMX512 (Digital Multiplex, a 1986 USITT standard, today maintained by ESTA/ANSI) is a unidirectional digital protocol that sends commands from the console to the equipment. 'Unidirectional' means the console speaks and the fixture just obeys — pure DMX receives no reply back (RDM is the bidirectional extension).
  • Universe, channels, and the 0–255 value: A DMX universe has 512 channels. Each channel carries a value from 0 to 255 (8 bits). Each parameter of a fixture (dimmer, red, green, blue, pan, tilt, gobo...) consumes one or more channels. A simple RGB LED PAR may use ~3 to 8 channels; a complex moving head may use 16, 24, or more. When 512 channels are not enough, more than one universe is used.
  • DMX cables and connectors: DMX travels over balanced cable with a 5-pin XLR connector (the official standard) — although a lot of market equipment uses 3-pin XLR. Important: DMX cable is a data cable (impedance ~120 Ω), different from microphone cable; using audio cable causes errors and flicker in large rigs. Fixtures are connected in a chain (daisy chain): the DMX OUT of one goes to the DMX IN of the next.
  • Chain topology and terminator: The signal enters the first fixture and goes out to the next, forming a line (not a star). At the end of the line a DMX terminator (120 Ω resistor on the last piece of equipment) is placed to absorb signal reflections and prevent random flickering/errors in long rigs. DMX splitters/boosters allow branching the line while keeping the signal clean.
  • Practical limits: Up to ~32 devices per line and good-quality cables are recommended; very long runs call for a booster. Ran out of the 512 channels? Open another universe — modern consoles control dozens or hundreds of universes via network nodes.
  • DMX over network: Art-Net and sACN: For many universes, DMX is transported over an Ethernet network with Art-Net or sACN (E1.31) protocols. The console sends everything over one network cable to a 'node,' which converts it back to several XLR outputs near the stage. This eliminates kilometers of cable and is standard in large productions. RDM, in turn, allows addressing and diagnosing fixtures remotely over the DMX line itself.

Equipment / details

  • DMX cable XLR 3/5-pin (120 Ω): Data cable specific to DMX. Connects console→fixture and fixture→fixture in a daisy chain. Not to be confused with microphone cable; in large rigs the impedance difference causes failures.
  • DMX terminator (120 Ω): A plug with a 120 Ω resistor inserted into the DMX OUT of the last fixture on the line. Absorbs reflections and stabilizes the signal on long chains.
  • DMX splitter / booster: Receives one DMX signal and distributes it isolated and amplified to several outputs. Used to branch the rig and protect the line from signal drops and shorts.
  • Art-Net / sACN node: Converter between Ethernet network and physical DMX outputs (XLR). Receives many universes over a network cable and distributes them on stage. Essential in large shows with hundreds of fixtures.

Step by step

  1. Build a simple DMX line: 1) DMX output of the console → DMX IN of fixture 1. 2) DMX OUT of fixture 1 → DMX IN of fixture 2, and so on. 3) On the DMX OUT of the last fixture, insert the 120 Ω terminator. 4) Use DMX cable (not audio) and avoid fragile splices.
  2. When to open a second universe: Add up the channels of all fixtures on the line. If it exceeds 512, move some of the fixtures to a second DMX output/universe of the console (or a node). Note which fixture is on which universe.
  3. Diagnose flicker: If the lights flicker randomly: check whether there is a terminator, whether the cable is DMX and not audio, whether the line does not exceed ~32 devices, and whether no connector is poorly seated. A splitter solves very heavily loaded lines.

In practice: At a festival with 180 fixtures, it would be impossible to run 6 long XLR cables from FOH (front of house) to the stage. The LD used the console sending 6 universes via sACN over a single network cable to an 8-port node on stage, which delivered each universe to its XLR output. Each line got a terminator and, where there were many fixtures, a splitter — zero flicker all night long.

Summary

  • DMX512 is unidirectional: the console commands, the fixture obeys; 512 channels per universe, values 0–255.
  • Daisy-chain connection with data cable and a 120 Ω terminator at the end of the line.
  • Art-Net and sACN carry many universes over a network; RDM allows remote addressing/diagnosing.

How the DMX signal travels

DMX512 is a series (daisy-chain) line: the cable leaves the console, enters and exits each fixture, one after another. At the end of the line goes a 120Ω terminator — without it, the signal reflects back and makes the last fixtures flicker/freeze. Each line supports up to ~32 devices; for more than that, or to create branches, use a splitter/booster (opto-isolated), which regenerates the signal into several independent outputs. And use real DMX cable (120Ω impedance) — microphone cable has the wrong impedance and causes intermittent errors.

Build the line in the simulator below and watch the signal health.

dfcb597c-9cf8-4053-94e1-dd2963598c17.titulo: Addressing and Patch: giving each fixture a 'zip code'
dfcb597c-9cf8-4053-94e1-dd2963598c17.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Addressing is the act that makes the right fixture light up. Get the address wrong, and you get the whole show wrong.

This is one of the most hands-on lessons of the course. You will understand what a fixture's DMX address is, how it relates to the number of channels it occupies, how to calculate the addresses of an entire rig without overlap, and what it means to 'do the patch' at the console — associating each physical address with a fixture profile (the 'personality'). It is step zero before any programming.

  • The DMX address (start address): Each fixture has a start address: the first channel of the universe it will 'listen' to. If an 8-channel fixture is at address 1, it occupies channels 1 to 8. The next one should start at 9 (1 + 8). The address is set physically on the fixture (display/buttons or old DIP switches) and replicated at the console.
  • Fixture mode and channel count: Many fixtures have several 'modes' (e.g., 8ch mode, 16ch, 24ch) that change how many channels they use and which functions are available. The mode chosen on the fixture MUST match the mode in the console's patch. Choose wrong and tilt becomes color, gobo becomes dimmer — chaos.
  • Calculating addresses without overlap: Golden rule: next address = current address + number of the fixture's channels. E.g.: 6 PARs of 8 channels → addresses 1, 9, 17, 25, 33, 41. Overlapping addresses makes two fixtures respond to the same command (sometimes useful on purpose, to clone). Unintentionally, it is an error.
  • DIP switches vs. digital display: Older equipment sets the address with binary switches (DIP switches): you add up the values of the switches that are on to form the address. Modern equipment has a display: you simply type the number. Knowing how to read DIP in binary is still useful in venues with legacy equipment.
  • What doing the PATCH is: Patch is telling the console: 'at address X, universe Y, there is a fixture of model Z in such-and-such mode.' The console loads the 'personality' (profile) of that manufacturer/model from its library, then knowing that channel 6 is color, 7 is gobo, etc. Without a correct patch, the console controls numbers, not 'intelligent lights.'
  • Personalities and the fixture library: Every console comes with a huge library of profiles (grandMA, MagicQ, Onyx, etc. download fixtures from the manufacturer's site). If your fixture is not on the list, you import the manufacturer's profile or create a custom one. Confirming that the profile's mode = the fixture's mode is the essential verification step.

Equipment / details

  • Fixture with address display: Modern moving heads and PARs show the address and mode on the display. You navigate the menu (Address / DMX Address) and type the number. It is where the physical address is set before it matches the patch.
  • Fixture with DIP switches: Legacy equipment (some PARs and strobes) uses banks of binary switches. Each switch is worth a power of 2 (1, 2, 4, 8, 16...); when on, they add up to the address. There are apps and tables that convert number↔DIP.
  • DMX tester / addressing app: Small testers or apps show what value is arriving on each channel, helping to check whether the fixture is responding at the expected address during setup.

Step by step

  1. Calculate the addresses of a rig: List the fixtures and how many channels each uses in the chosen mode. Start at 1. For each following fixture, add the previous one's channel count. E.g.: 4 moving heads of 16ch → 1, 17, 33, 49. Note it in a table (fixture, model, mode, universe, address).
  2. Address a fixture physically: 1) On the display, enter the address menu (Address/DMX). 2) Type the calculated address (e.g.: 17). 3) Confirm the mode (e.g.: 16ch standard). 4) Repeat for all, following your table.
  3. Do the patch at the console: 1) On the patch screen, choose the library and select the exact model and mode. 2) Enter the universe and start address identical to the fixture's. 3) Assign a logical fixture/channel number (e.g.: Fixture 1). 4) Repeat; many consoles patch in batches (4 fixtures starting from address 1, step of 16).
  4. Verify the patch: Select each fixture at the console and open the dimmer/color. Confirm that the correct physical light responds. If the wrong one lights up, the address or mode is swapped — fix it before programming.

In practice: For a show with 4 moving spots (16ch each) and 8 LED PARs (8ch each), the operator built the table: spots at addresses 1, 17, 33, 49; PARs at addresses 65, 73, 81, 89, 97, 105, 113, 121. He addressed each fixture on the display, did the patch matching model and mode, and tested them one by one by opening the dimmer. One PAR lit up wrong: it was in 6ch mode on the fixture and 8ch in the patch. He fixed the mode and moved on to programming.

Summary

  • The DMX address is the fixture's 'zip code'; it and the mode must match between fixture and patch.
  • Next address = current address + the fixture's number of channels (without overlapping).
  • Patch associates each address with the correct personality so the console controls functions, not just numbers.

Addressing: each fixture's zip code

A DMX universe has 512 channels. Each fixture occupies a range of channels starting from its start address, the size of its mode (number of channels): a moving head may use 16 channels, an LED PAR 4. If you address fixture A at 1, it occupies 1–16; the next should start at 17. Addressing two in the same range causes a conflict — both receive the same commands and fight over the channel. The address is set in the digital menu or via DIP switches (in binary: 1,2,4,8,16…). Add up the switches that are on to find the address.

Practice in the simulator below: resolve the conflicts and use the DIP switches.

34e6aa66-1ef3-4813-92fd-3480b74a5b6b.titulo: Moving heads: Spot, Beam, and Wash
34e6aa66-1ef3-4813-92fd-3480b74a5b6b.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The 'robots' of the stage. Understanding when to use Spot, Beam, or Wash is what gives your design its identity.

Moving heads are the most striking intelligent fixtures of a show. They rotate in pan (horizontal) and tilt (vertical) and change color, focus, and pattern via DMX. There are three big families — Spot, Beam, and Wash — and hybrids that combine everything. This lesson details what each one does, their typical DMX parameters, and when to choose each.

  • Pan, tilt, and the moving head: Every moving head has a head that rotates: pan (horizontal, typically 540°) and tilt (vertical, ~270°). These movements are controlled by DMX channels and allow you to 'draw' in the air and sweep the stage. The precision and speed of the motor define the fluidity of choreographed effects.
  • Moving Wash: Emits broad, soft light to 'bathe' large areas with color. Usually LED RGBW/RGBA, with zoom to open/close the beam. Ideal for coloring the stage, creating atmospheres, and smooth color transitions. It does not create sharp patterns — its strength is coverage and color mixing.
  • Moving Beam: Produces a narrow, intense, parallel beam ('laser-like' ray) that cuts through the air, especially with smoke/haze. Uses a discharge lamp or high-density LED, prism, and sometimes ring gobos. It is the classic 'aerial' festival and EDM effect. Very tight beam angle (a few degrees).
  • Moving Spot: Versatile: projects gobos (patterns/textures/logos), has an iris, adjustable focus, color wheel, and often a prism. Creates sharp patterns on the floor, on the set, and in the air. It is the 'Swiss Army knife' — used for projecting shapes, cutouts, and defined effects.
  • Hybrids and common DMX parameters: Many current models are hybrids (Spot+Beam+Wash). Typical DMX parameters: pan/tilt (and their 'fine' versions for precision), dimmer, shutter/strobe, color (wheel or RGBW), gobo (fixed and rotating wheel), focus, zoom, prism, frost, control/reset. The more functions, the more channels.
  • When to use each one: Wash for color and coverage; Beam for aerial effect and impact; Spot for patterns and projection. A balanced rig usually combines all three: washes fill, beams create the 'air show,' spots draw. The choice depends on the genre, the size of the stage, and the budget.

Equipment / details

  • Moving Head Wash (LED RGBW, with zoom): Bathes large areas with color with a broad beam and smooth mixing. Use to color the stage and background, create moods, and color transitions. Check the zoom range and the number of LEDs/power.
  • Moving Head Beam (discharge or LED, narrow beam): Creates intense, parallel rays that cut through the air with smoke. Use for aerial effect, EDM/festival looks, and rhythmic impact. Watch out for heat and power draw of discharge versions.
  • Moving Head Spot (gobos, prism, focus, iris): Projects patterns, textures, and logos with sharp edges. Use to draw on the floor/set and for cutouts. Evaluate the number of rotating and fixed gobos and the focus quality.
  • Hybrid Moving Head (Spot+Beam+Wash): Combines the three functions in a single fixture, saving points and weight on the truss. Excellent value in compact rigs; check whether each mode delivers enough quality for your needs.

Step by step

  1. Identify the type of moving head: Look at the beam: wide and diffuse = Wash; narrow and hard that 'scratches the air' = Beam; with a front lens and gobos projecting patterns = Spot. Confirm on the datasheet the beam angle (degrees) and the features (gobo, prism, zoom).
  2. Map the parameters in the patch: After the patch, open the fixture's channel view at the console and locate pan, tilt, dimmer, color, gobo, focus, zoom, and prism. Move each one in isolation to understand what it controls — a basis for creating palettes later.
  3. Choose the fixture for the goal: Need to color the entire stage? Wash. Want rays in the air on the drop of the song? Beam. Going to project the artist's logo on the backdrop screen? Spot. Tight budget and few points on the truss? Hybrid.

In practice: At an electronic show, the LD set up 8 beams on the back bar for aerial rays synced to the beat, 6 washes to flood the stage with color at every drop, and 4 spots to project rotating gobos on the floor during the breaks. On the drop, all the beams rose together forming a fan — an effect impossible with washes, which do not tighten the beam.

Summary

  • Wash bathes color, Beam cuts through the air, Spot projects patterns; hybrids do all three.
  • Pan/tilt move the head; color, gobo, focus, zoom, and prism shape the beam.
  • A balanced rig combines all three types according to genre and stage.

Spot, Beam, and Wash: when to use each

Moving heads are divided by beam type. The Spot has a defined beam and accepts a gobo (projects designs/textures), zoom, iris, focus, and prism — it is the versatile mid-stage fixture. The Beam has a columnar, thin, and intense beam (small angle, ~3–7°) that rips through the air (aerial effect, great with smoke) and makes fans with a prism. The Wash opens a broad, soft bath of color, with no hard edge or gobo — it serves to fill the stage with color. Many hybrid fixtures (Spot+Beam+Wash) do all three.

Compare the three in the simulator below (switch the type and adjust zoom, gobo, prism, and color).

3d88e308-a15f-438d-bb28-3a98e1a6e85f.titulo: LED PAR, footlight, COB, and wash-of-light sources
3d88e308-a15f-438d-bb28-3a98e1a6e85f.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Not every hero moves. Static fixtures are the silent backbone of almost every rig.

Before the movers, every stage needs a base of light: the static wash fixtures. LED PARs, footlights (cyc/background wash), COBs, and brute fixtures do the 'heavy' work of color and coverage. This lesson explains each one, their technologies, and where they fit in the design — including the concept of RGB additive color mixing.

  • LED PAR: 'PAR' comes from Parabolic Aluminized Reflector. The modern LED PAR uses LEDs (RGB, RGBW, RGBWA+UV) in place of the lamp, doing away with gels: the color comes out via DMX. It is efficient, cool, durable, and versatile — the most common fixture in any rig. Used to light the stage, background, audience, and create color moods.
  • Additive color mixing (RGB+): In LED, color is born from the sum of red, green, and blue lights (additive mixing): R+G+B = white; R+G = yellow; G+B = cyan. The W (white) channel improves pastel tones and true white; A (amber) and UV expand the palette. Understanding this is the basis for creating faithful color palettes.
  • Footlight (cyc light / background wash): A wide, flat fixture, with LEDs or lamps arranged horizontally, designed to light a cyclorama, backdrops, and vertical surfaces with uniform color, without a hard shadow. Creates 'walls of color' and gradients — fundamental in theater, TV, and stages with a backdrop.
  • COB LED (Chip-on-Board): Several LED chips mounted together on a single board, generating a dense, uniform source of light, often used as a blinder/audience light of intense warm light or as a powerful wash. The proximity of the chips gives homogeneous mixing and high brightness with good heat control.
  • Brute and blinders: 'Brute' designates a high-power fixture (several lamps/sources) to light large areas or blast the audience (blinder). In shows, banks of blinders become an effect in themselves, 'blinding' the audience at the climax. Modern versions use COB or high-power lamps.
  • When to use static fixtures: Use static fixtures as a base of color and coverage (the 'canvas' on which the movers draw), for background/cyclorama (footlight), for impact blinders (COB/brute), and where no movement is needed — saving weight, power draw, and budget.

Equipment / details

  • LED PAR (RGBW / RGBWA+UV): Static wash fixture with color via DMX. Use for color moods, lighting the background, audience, and as the rig's base. Check the number of channels, beam angle, and whether it has W for decent whites.
  • Footlight / Cyc light: Wide wash for backdrops and cycloramas, uniform color without hard shadow. Use for walls of color, gradients, and background atmospheres in theater, TV, and shows.
  • COB LED / blinder: Dense, uniform, high-brightness source. Use as a powerful wash or warm-light audience blinder. Good mixing and heat control; great for impact moments.
  • Brute / bank of blinders: High-power fixture for large areas or to 'blast' the audience at the climax. Requires care with heat, power draw, and electrical safety.

Step by step

  1. Create a color by additive mixing: On an RGBW fixture, raise R and G together for yellow; G and B for cyan; R and B for magenta; all three for white. Add W to soften/brighten. Note the values that generate the colors of your palette.
  2. Set up a static color base: Position front and back PARs covering the stage. Set a base color (e.g.: warm amber) and use footlights for the cyclorama. This layer becomes the 'backdrop' on which the movers act.
  3. Use blinders responsibly: Reserve COB/brute for peaks (chorus, drop). Program a quick bump and avoid keeping the audience dazzled for too long. Be careful with heat near people and scenery.

In practice: At a wedding, the technician set up 12 LED PARs lighting the hall's walls in warm amber (high R, medium G, W to soften), 4 footlights tinting the backdrop behind the couple in soft pink, and 2 COBs as a highlight on the dance floor. At party time, the COBs became warm blinders on the choruses. No fixture moved — and the environment came out flawless and economical.

Summary

  • The LED PAR is the most common static fixture; color comes out via RGB(W) additive mixing.
  • The footlight bathes backdrops and cycloramas with uniform color; the COB gives dense, powerful light.
  • Brute/blinders create impact on the audience — use with thermal and visual responsibility.

LED PAR and COB: color wash by additive mixing

The LED PAR does a colored wash of light by mixing the color LEDs. In additive mixing, the lights add up: R+G=yellow, G+B=cyan, R+B=magenta, R+G+B=white. The White channel brightens and creates pastel tones; the Amber produces the warm/tungsten tone that RGB alone does not reach well (which is why many are RGBW, RGBWA, or RGBWA+UV). The COB (chip-on-board) joins the chips into a single source — more homogeneous color and a single shadow (good for a beam), while the classic multi-LED shows dots and multi-shadows.

Mix the colors in the simulator below and hit the targets.

ba321ead-24e7-49df-bdee-0fbcf6130136.titulo: Effects: strobe, atomic, laser, smoke/hazer and gobos
ba321ead-24e7-49df-bdee-0fbcf6130136.conteudo_rico:
To get started: These are the show's seasoning. Used in the right dose, they electrify; in excess, they tire out and even harm the audience.

Special effects deliver the visual 'punch': the strobe that freezes motion, the laser that slices through the air, the smoke that makes light visible, the gobos that project patterns. This lesson introduces each effect, how it works, and — crucially — the safety and health precautions, especially with strobe (photosensitive epilepsy) and laser (eyesight).

  • Strobe and the stroboscopic effect: The strobe emits intense, rapid flashes (xenon or high-power LED) at an adjustable frequency, creating the illusion of frozen or accelerated motion. It energizes choruses and drops. WARNING: it can cause discomfort, migraines and even seizures in photosensitive people — use it in moderation and warn the audience at sensitive events.
  • Atomic / high-power strobe: The 'Atomic' (popularized by Martin's line and used as a generic term for powerful strobes) is a very-high-output strobe, sometimes with its own color channel and dimmer, capable of blinding pulses. Be careful: the source material notes that 'atomic' is also used as an adjective for any very intense effect — always check the actual model.
  • Laser: It emits monochromatic, coherent and highly concentrated light, creating beams, fans and sharp geometric patterns in the air. Controlled by DMX. It is rated by hazard class (classes I to IV); show equipment is usually class IIIb/IV and REQUIRES strict precautions: never point it at the audience's eyes, respect the minimum sweep height and local regulations.
  • Smoke machine vs. hazer: A smoke machine heats a fluid (glycol/glycerine-based) to generate dense, visible fog — great for 'clouds' and revealing beams at specific moments. The hazer produces a fine, homogeneous mist that stays suspended, leaving ALL of the air slightly diffuse and making beams and lasers visible throughout the whole show. For continuous aerial rays, the hazer is ideal.
  • Gobos: A gobo is a disc/plate with a cut-out pattern (metal or glass) placed in the light path to project shapes, textures, logos and patterns. In moving spots there is a wheel of fixed and rotating gobos. Combined with prism and focus, they create everything from foliage and windows to brand logos.
  • Safety and health with effects: Strobe and laser are the effects with the greatest health risk. Signal the use of a strobe (an epilepsy trigger). Ensure ventilation when using smoke (it can cause respiratory discomfort). Lasers require a qualified operator and legal compliance. The rule: the effect serves the narrative, not the operator's ego.

Equipment / details

  • LED or xenon strobe / Atomic: Intense, rapid flashes for choruses and drops. Adjust frequency and intensity. Photosensitivity risk — use sparingly and warn sensitive audiences.
  • Show laser (class IIIb/IV): Coherent beams and geometric patterns via DMX. Requires a qualified operator, safe sweeping above the audience and legal compliance. Never point it at the eyes.
  • Smoke machine: Dense, localized fog to reveal beams and create 'clouds'. Glycol-based fluid. Ensure ventilation; it disperses quickly.
  • Hazer: Fine, homogeneous mist that stays suspended, leaving the air diffuse throughout the show. Ideal for continuous beams and lasers. Lower fluid consumption and a 'cleaner' effect than smoke.
  • Gobos (fixed and rotating): Discs with patterns to project textures, shapes and logos via moving spots. Glass (colored/detailed) or metal (cut-outs). Combine with prism and focus.

Step by step

  1. Reveal beams with a hazer: Before the show, turn on the hazer and let the air 'charge' for a few minutes. Adjust the density so the beams are visible without 'washing out' the stage. Top it up throughout the night as the air clears.
  2. Program a safe strobe: Create a strobe effect on a separate button/cuelist, with controlled frequency. Trigger it only in short peaks. At events with a sensitive audience, avoid it or give a warning; never leave the strobe running continuously for long periods.
  3. Project and adjust a gobo: On the spot, select the gobo wheel, choose the pattern, adjust the focus until the edge is sharp (or intentionally soft), add rotation and a prism if you want to multiply the pattern. Aim it at the target with pan/tilt.

In practice: At an EDM festival, the operator kept the hazer maintaining uniformly diffuse air all night, which made the beams appear as solid rays. He reserved the strobe and the lasers only for the drops: lasers sweeping above the heads (never in the eyes) and strobe in short bursts. For a melodic break, he projected slow rotating gobos on the floor — alternating energy and breathing room without tiring the audience.

Summary

  • Strobe and laser are the highest health-risk effects — they demand moderation and legal compliance.
  • The hazer (fine, continuous mist) is the secret to keeping beams and lasers visible throughout the whole show.
  • Gobos project patterns, textures and logos; focus, prism and rotation expand the possibilities.

Light effects: an overview

Beyond the fixtures, there are the effects that create impact: the strobe (pulses that freeze motion — watch out for photosensitivity), the atomic/blinder (a flash into the audience on the turnarounds), the laser (beams and patterns in the air — never aim at the audience; requires certification), the smoke/hazer (the air that reveals the beams) and the gobos (masks that project designs and textures). You already operated smoke/hazer in the Atmosphere Machines lesson, and gobos in the Moving Heads simulator. Here, focus on the strobe and the laser — those with the greatest impact and the greatest safety responsibility.

Try the strobe and laser in the simulator below (strobe preview dimmed for safety).

1054e10e-5a93-48db-9024-a52225672719.titulo: Dimmers, splitters and the power and data chain
1054e10e-5a93-48db-9024-a52225672719.conteudo_rico:
To get started: Between the outlet and the light there is a whole chain. Knowing it is what keeps the show from 'going dark' at the wrong moment.

For a fixture to light up and obey, two signals must arrive: power (AC) and data (DMX). This lesson closes the equipment module by detailing the power and data chain — dimmers (and the difference versus 'non-dim' LED fixtures), power distribution, DMX splitters and correct cabling.

  • What a dimmer is: A dimmer is the device that controls light intensity by varying the electrical power delivered. It was essential in the era of incandescent/halogen lamps: the console sent DMX to a dimmer rack, which regulated the lamps' voltage (conventional 'dimmable' lamps). There, the DMX channel controls the dimmer, not the lamp directly.
  • Conventional (dimmer) vs. LED (non-dim): Conventional fixtures need an external dimmer. Intelligent/LED fixtures, on the other hand, have an internal dimmer: they receive AC directly (constant supply, 'non-dim') and control intensity through their own dimmer channel in DMX. That's why moving heads and LED PARs plug into constant-power outlets, not dimmed circuits.
  • Dimmer rack and power distribution: In rigs with conventionals, a dimmer rack is used (several power channels) fed by three-phase power, receiving DMX from the console. The distribution (power distro) splits the power into circuits protected by breakers, balancing the phases so none is overloaded.
  • DMX splitter / booster: The DMX splitter receives a signal and replicates it, isolated and amplified, across several outputs. It serves to branch the rig (several lines from a single console output), protect against a short on one line and reinforce the signal over long runs — avoiding flicker.
  • Cabling: data and power kept separate: DMX cable (data, ~120 Ω) and AC cable (power) serve different functions and don't mix. Good practices: use proper connectors (XLR for DMX; powerCON/Schuko/industrial for AC), avoid running data alongside power cables (interference), leave a service loop at the connectors (no strain) and label and coil cables neatly.
  • Power-up and power-down sequence: Power up the fixtures and let them initialize (movings do a reset/homing); then power on/connect the console and check the DMX. To power down, black out on the console before cutting the power, and de-energize in stages to avoid surges. Never yank AC from hot fixtures carelessly.

Equipment / details

  • Dimmer rack: Controls the intensity of conventional fixtures (incandescent/halogen) by varying power, commanded by DMX. In LED-only rigs it is unnecessary. Fed by three-phase power.
  • Power distro (power distributor): Splits the supply into several protected circuits, balancing phases. It centralizes the breakers and makes safe rig-load sizing easier.
  • DMX splitter / booster: Replicates the DMX signal across several isolated and amplified outputs. It branches the rig, isolates line faults and reinforces the signal over long distances, avoiding flicker.
  • AC cables (powerCON / industrial) and DMX (XLR): AC carries power; DMX carries data. Use the right connector for each, keep them organized and avoid running data alongside power to reduce interference.

Step by step

  1. Build the chain for an LED fixture: 1) AC: connect the fixture to a constant-power circuit (non-dim) with the correct voltage. 2) DMX: run a data cable from the console (or splitter) to the DMX IN and continue in a daisy chain. 3) Address/patch as in the Module 2 lessons. 4) Test by opening the dimmer on the console.
  2. Decide whether you need a dimmer: Conventional fixture (a regular dimmable lamp)? It needs a dimmer rack between the power and the fixture, commanded by DMX. LED/intelligent fixture? Do NOT use an external dimmer — feed it constant power and control intensity via the DMX dimmer channel.
  3. Balance the phases: On the power distro, add up the watts per phase and distribute the fixtures so no phase gets overloaded. Check the current with a clamp meter before the show.

In practice: At a venue with a large stage, the technician ran a single DMX cable from the console to a 4-output splitter on stage: one line for the rear movings, another for the front ones, another for the PARs and one spare. All the LEDs were connected to constant power via the distro (never to a dimmer), with the fixtures distributed across the three phases. Result: stable signal, no flicker, and no overloaded phase.

Summary

  • Every fixture needs two signals: power (AC) and data (DMX).
  • Conventionals use an external dimmer; LEDs/movings receive constant power and dim via DMX.
  • A DMX splitter and power distro keep data stable and power balanced and safe.

Power and data: dimmer vs. direct power

They are two parallel chains. The data one (DMX) leaves the console and reaches the fixtures via a splitter. The power one depends on the type of fixture: a conventional one (a tungsten lamp — PAR 64, Fresnel) has no electronics, so it goes in the dimmer pack, which chops the sine wave to vary intensity (the DMX commands the dimmer). Meanwhile intelligent ones (moving heads and LED) have their own switching power supply and dim themselves: they need direct, constant power (from the distro). Connecting a moving head/LED to a dimmer channel is a serious mistake — the chopped sine wave burns out the power supply. Also: respect the load (watts) of each bus/circuit and balance the phases.

Route it correctly in the simulator below and watch the load.

817c7a4a-564e-4ab8-9547-9c7e73817359.titulo: Types of console and the main brands/models
817c7a4a-564e-4ab8-9547-9c7e73817359.conteudo_rico:
To get started: The console is your instrument. Knowing the console families helps you speak the language of any venue.

There are many consoles, but they share a common logic. This lesson classifies the types (physical, PC-based, touch, hybrid, portable) and presents the real brands and models most used in the market, showing what kind of work each platform is usually chosen for. The goal is for you to recognize any console and know where to look for what you need — always consulting the manufacturer's manual.

  • Types of console: Traditional (physical desks with faders and buttons, common in theater and fixed installations); PC-based (software on a computer, controlled by mouse/keyboard/surface); touchscreen; hybrid (physical faders + touch); and portable/compact (for touring shows and small events). The current trend is hybrid: tactile hardware + powerful software.
  • MA Lighting (grandMA): The grandMA2 and grandMA3 family (Full-Size, Light, Compact) is a global reference for major tours, festivals and TV. Its logic is based on executors, sequences and a very deep programming engine. The grandMA3 onPC lets you run the software on a PC with command wings. It is the market standard for big shows.
  • ChamSys MagicQ: The MagicQ line (e.g., MQ80, MQ500 Stadium) and the free MagicQ software for PC. Very popular for its value and for letting you start on software alone and migrate to the hardware later. Strong for busking and for medium/large rigs.
  • Avolites: Titan is the brand's operating system (Tiger Touch, Quartz, and the Titan Mobile/Go surface that runs on PC). Known for a nimble approach to programming and busking, with a strong presence in band shows and tours.
  • Obsidian Onyx and ETC: Obsidian Control Systems uses the Onyx software (NX consoles) — successor to the Martin M-PC, with a free version to get started. ETC (Eos Ti, Ion Xe, Element, ColorSource) dominates the theater world, with a 'tracking' cue- and channel-based workflow, ideal for shows with many precise cues.
  • Other brands and the choice criterion: High End (Hog 4), Jands (Vista), Zero 88 (FLX) and others coexist in the market. The choice depends on the size of the event, the genre (theater favors ETC; concert/festival favors grandMA/Avo/ChamSys), the budget and — very much so — what the operator already knows. Rule: always study the manual of the specific model before the show.

Equipment / details

  • MA Lighting grandMA2 / grandMA3: The reference console for big shows, festivals and TV. Deep programming via executors and sequences; grandMA3 onPC runs on a computer. When the event is large and complex, it is the standard for many LDs.
  • ChamSys MagicQ (MQ80, MQ500): A platform with free software for PC and dedicated consoles. Excellent value and strong for busking; a great entry point since it lets you learn on the software before buying hardware.
  • Avolites Titan (Tiger Touch, Titan Mobile): A nimble system for live programming and busking, popular on band tours. Titan Mobile/Go runs on PC with a USB surface.
  • Obsidian Onyx (NX) and ETC Eos/Ion: Onyx: software (with a free version) and NX consoles, good for clubs and medium size. ETC Eos/Ion/Element: the theater standard, a 'tracking' cue workflow with very high cue precision.

Step by step

  1. Identify the venue's console: On arrival, identify the exact brand and model. Locate the manual (online or on the console itself). Recognize the universal blocks: the fixture selection area, faders/executors, the programming area (Record/Store), playback and the patch screens.
  2. Choose the platform for a project: A festival show with many movings and effects? grandMA/Avolites/ChamSys. A theater piece with 200 precise cues? ETC Eos/Ion. A small event/tight budget? MagicQ or Onyx on PC with a cheap surface. Always weigh what you know.
  3. Train for free before buying hardware: Download MagicQ or Onyx (free) or grandMA3 onPC and practice in offline/visualizer mode. You learn the logic of patch, groups, palettes and cuelists without needing the physical console.

In practice: An operator was hired for a festival whose venue only had grandMA3, but he came from ChamSys. Instead of improvising, he downloaded grandMA3 onPC days beforehand, studied the official manual and practiced patching and creating executors in the visualizer. On the day, he recognized the logic (sequences/executors) and operated with confidence — proving that understanding the standard workflow is worth more than memorizing a single console.

Summary

  • Consoles vary in form, but share the same logic: select, program, play back.
  • grandMA, Avolites and ChamSys dominate shows; ETC dominates theater with precise cues.
  • Several platforms have free software for PC — train the logic before tackling the hardware.

The screens and workflow of each console

Each console has an operating philosophy. On the grandMA (MA Lighting) you work by command line and executors: you select the fixtures in the sheet, give At Full, adjust the encoders (Dimmer/Pan/Tilt/Color) and Store to an executor. On Avolites (Titan) the workflow is Locate → adjust attributes → Record to playbacks and palettes. A basic DMX console, meanwhile, is the foundation of everything: 1 fader = 1 DMX channel (one dimmer). Understanding all three gives you the base to operate any venue.

Operate all three in the simulator below (switch the brand at the top).

🎚️ grandMA onPC Lab — train on MA's REAL software

The simulator above is for quick practice in the browser. To go deeper, MA itself offers grandMA3 onPC (and grandMA2 onPC) for free — it is the same software as the console, with no reduced version, running on your Windows PC. This is how professionals pre-program a show at home.

1) Download the software (free, MA's official site)

  • grandMA3 onPC (recommended, current): malighting.com/downloads (https://www.malighting.com/downloads/) → grandMA3 tab → Software + Release Notes → grandMA3 onPC Software for Windows. Overview: grandMA3 onPC page (https://www.malighting.com/special/grandma3-onpc/).
  • grandMA2 onPC (if the venue still uses MA2): grandMA2 downloads (https://www.malighting.com/downloads/products/grandma2/).
  • Official Windows installation guide: help.malighting.com — Windows Installation (https://help.malighting.com/grandMA3/2.0/HTML/onpc_windows_installation.html).

Honest note: onPC runs on the student's computer (Windows), not inside this page — that's why we download it straight from MA (always the latest version and with their registration). The simulator here on the platform is your quick training in the browser.

2) Minimum setup

Install it, open onPC, create a New Show. Optional: download MA 3D (the visualizer) to see the fixtures react in 3D while you program.

3) Guided exercises (do them in onPC, mirroring the simulator)

  1. Patch: add 8 moving heads and address them in DMX (just like the Addressing lesson) — the 1st at 1, the 2nd right after.
  2. Selection + intensity: select the fixtures, At Full, adjust the Dimmer on the encoder.
  3. Groups and presets: create a Group with the movings and color/position presets.
  4. Store + playback: build a look, Store it to an Executor and bring up the fader — just like you did in the simulator.
  5. Cuelist: record 3 cues and run the sequence with Go.

When these steps feel natural in onPC, you are operating MA for real. Come back to the simulator here whenever you want to review the quick workflow.

91591c48-6a2f-4b35-95bd-6a7c311009e3.conteudo_rico:
To get started: Programming light is organizing shortcuts. Groups and palettes are what make a big show operable by one person.

Every modern console follows a similar flow: you select fixtures, give a parameter (color, position, gobo, intensity), and store that state. To avoid repeating work, you create GROUPS (sets of fixtures) and PALETTES/presets (reusable states of color, position and beam). This lesson teaches that universal logic and the selection syntax that recurs on most consoles.

  • The universal flow: select → adjust → store: Almost every console follows: 1) select the fixtures; 2) adjust the parameters (dimmer, color, pan/tilt, gobo, etc.); 3) record (Record/Store) to a group, palette, scene or cue. The buttons change names across brands, but the logic is the same. Mastering this cycle is mastering any console.
  • Selection syntax (command line): Many consoles have a command line with logic like '1 THRU 8 ENTER' (selects fixtures 1 to 8), '+' to add, '-' to exclude. Encoders (knobs) adjust the parameters of the active attribute (color, position...). Learning to select quickly is what gives you agility in busking.
  • Groups: A group is a saved set of fixtures (e.g., 'all the rear beams', 'front PARs', 'odd movings'). Instead of selecting them one by one every time, you call the group with one touch. Good groups (by type, by position, by odd/even) radically speed up operation.
  • Color palettes: A color palette/preset stores a ready-made color (e.g., 'warm amber', 'magenta', 'cyan'). You create it once and reapply it to any fixture/scene. A huge advantage: if you change the palette's definition, all scenes that use it update together (live reference). Standardize the palette at the start of programming.
  • Position palettes: Position palettes store pan/tilt combinations (e.g., 'lead singer', 'drums', 'center stage', 'audience'). Instead of aiming manually every time, you call the position. Create positions for the stage's key points right at focus time — it is the foundation of all clean movement.
  • Beam/gobo palettes and the live reference: In the same way, store gobos, prisms, focus and zoom as beam palettes. The great power of palettes is that they are referenced: scenes point to the palette, not to fixed values. If the stage changes size, you just readjust the position in the palette and all the scenes stay correct.

Equipment / details

  • Console with encoders and attribute screens: Most professional consoles have encoders (knobs) that change function according to the selected attribute (color, position, gobo, beam) and screens that display presets/palettes. Use them to adjust parameters and store palettes.
  • Surface/PC wing: Wings and USB surfaces add physical faders and buttons to PC software (MagicQ, Onyx, Avolites Go, grandMA3 onPC), allowing groups and palettes on physical buttons without a complete console.

Step by step

  1. Create a group: 1) Select the desired fixtures (e.g., '1 THRU 8' or by clicking). 2) Trigger Record/Store Group. 3) Touch a free group button and name it (e.g., 'Rear Beams'). 4) Test by calling the group and opening the dimmer.
  2. Create a color palette: 1) Select fixtures (preferably all of the same type). 2) Adjust the desired color on the encoders/picker. 3) Record/Store in the color palette area. 4) Name it ('Warm Amber'). 5) Reapply it to other fixtures to confirm.
  3. Create a position palette: 1) Select the movings. 2) Aim pan/tilt at the target (e.g., lead singer). 3) Store as a position palette and name it. 4) Repeat for 'Drums', 'Center', 'Audience'. Use only pan/tilt in the preset to keep it reusable.
  4. Combine group + palettes: Call the 'Rear Beams' group, apply the 'Cyan' palette and the 'Open Fan' position. In three touches you have a ready look — and this is exactly how you operate quickly live.

In practice: Before a show, the operator created groups ('Front', 'Rear', 'Spots', 'Washes', 'Odds', 'Evens'), an 8-color palette and positions for lead singer, guitar, drums, center and audience. At night, while busking, he built looks in seconds: 'Rear' + 'Magenta' + 'Fan', then 'Spots' + 'White' + 'Lead Singer'. When the stage was reset slightly differently the next day, he just readjusted the 'Lead Singer' position in the palette and everything focused correctly again.

Summary

  • Every console follows the cycle: select fixtures, adjust parameters, store the state.
  • Groups gather fixtures; palettes store reusable color, position and beam.
  • Palettes are a live reference — change the palette and all scenes that use it follow.

The grandMA navigation logic

MA works by selection + action and by pools (banks of tiles). You select fixtures — directly in the sheet or via a Group (a saved group: All, odd/even movings, etc.) — and then apply actions: intensity (At Full or the Dimmer encoder), position (the Pan/Tilt encoders) and color (Colour Presets or the encoders). The palettes/presets store ready-made values (a color, a position) to reuse. When the look is good, you Store it to a Sequence/Executor, and the executor fader plays that look on stage (HTP). Everything appears in the command line at the top — MA is commanded by language (Fixture, At, Group, Store…).

Train this navigation on the interface replica below (it's the web trainer; the real, free software is in the "grandMA onPC Lab").

c906f979-72c0-4466-9dee-73c36ee42969.titulo: Scenes, cues and cuelists: from static state to the whole show
c906f979-72c0-4466-9dee-73c36ee42969.conteudo_rico:
To get started: A scene is a photograph of light. A cuelist is the movie. Here you learn to build the show from beginning to end.

With fixtures patched and groups and palettes ready, you move on to building the show: scenes (saved light states), cues (cues with transition times) and cuelists (sequences of cues that run in order). This lesson covers how to record scenes, set fade/delay, chain cues into a list, and the concepts of tracking vs. cue-only that distinguish the theater workflow from the concert one.

  • Scene (look) and cue: A scene is a recorded light state: which fixtures, at what intensity, color, position and gobo. A cue is that scene within a sequence, with times: fade in, fade out, delay and sometimes per-attribute times. Pressing GO runs the next cue with its programmed transition.
  • Times: fade, delay and crossfade: Fade is the transition time (e.g., 3 s for the light to change smoothly). Delay postpones the start. Crossfade is the smooth passage from one look to another. Well-chosen times are the difference between an elegant turnaround and a 'hard cut' — each cue can have its own.
  • Cuelist (list of cues): A cuelist is the ordered sequence of cues, run with GO (advance), GO BACK (return) and sometimes GOTO (jump). It is the backbone of theater shows and of concerts with a fixed setlist: cue 1 opening, cue 2 song 1, cue 3 chorus... The operator just follows the cue by pressing GO.
  • Playbacks, executors and faders: Cues and cuelists live in playbacks (faders/buttons, called executors on some consoles). You can have the main cuelist on a fader and standalone effects (strobe, blinder, chase) on other buttons to trigger live. Organizing the playbacks well is halfway to a smooth show.
  • Tracking vs. Cue-Only: In tracking mode (typical of ETC/theater), a value remains in the following cues until it is changed — efficient for long sequences. In cue-only mode, each cue stores a complete, isolated state. Knowing which mode the console is in avoids the classic 'I changed cue 5 and it messed up cue 30'.
  • Chases and effects within cues: A chase is an automatic rhythmic sequence (e.g., PARs flashing in order) with adjustable speed/BPM. Effects (the effect engine) generate cyclic movements and variations (pan/tilt circles, color waves). They can be recorded within cues or kept in separate playbacks for busking.

Equipment / details

  • Console with playbacks/executors and a GO button: Faders and buttons that play back cuelists and cues, with GO/GO BACK. The heart of live operation: the main cuelist on a playback and effects on others. Each brand names it differently, but the function is the same.
  • The console's effect engine / chase: An internal engine that creates cyclic movements and variations (circles, waves, color/dimmer chases) adjustable in size, speed and phase. It greatly speeds up creating dynamic looks without programming frame by frame.

Step by step

  1. Record a scene/cue: 1) Build the look (groups + palettes + intensities). 2) Record/Store. 3) Direct it to the cuelist and the desired cue number. 4) Set the fade time (e.g., 3 s). 5) Clear the programming and test with GO.
  2. Chain a cuelist: 1) Record cue 1 (opening). 2) Build the next look and record it as cue 2, with its fade. 3) Repeat, creating cues in order. 4) Adjust fades/delays cue by cue. 5) Run the whole list with GO from start to finish to validate the transitions.
  3. Add an effect/chase: 1) Select a group. 2) Open the effect engine and choose an effect (e.g., a pan/tilt circle or a dimmer chase). 3) Adjust size, speed and phase. 4) Record it within a cue or on a separate playback to trigger live.
  4. Organize the playbacks for the show: Put the main cuelist on a central fader. Distribute onto the buttons: strobe, blinder/bump, color chase, blackout. Label everything. That way, during the show, you just follow the cuelist and season it with the effects.

In practice: For a theater musical, the LD programmed a cuelist with 120 cues following the script: each of the director's cues became a cue with a precise fade (opening in 5 s, a hard blackout in 0 s at the end of a scene). In tracking mode, the scene's base light remained between cues, and he only changed what changed. The operator ran the entire show pressing GO on the cues marked in the script — repeatable night after night with precision.

Summary

  • A scene is a light state; a cue is that state with times; a cuelist is the sequence run with GO.
  • Fade, delay and crossfade define the elegance of the transitions; each cue has its own.
  • Tracking propagates values between cues; cue-only isolates each one — know which mode you're in.

From static state to the whole show

A scene/look is a state of the light at one instant. A cue is that scene saved with a fade time. A cuelist (sequence) is the list of cues in order: by pressing GO, the console crossfades from the current scene to the next in the programmed time — and so the whole show runs with one finger, always the same. Short fade times give hard turnarounds; long ones, smooth transitions. You record each cue when the look is ready and adjust the fade of each passage. It is the heart of theater operation and of shows with a timeline.

Run the cuelist in the simulator below: press GO, change the fade time and record your own cues.

21733fd6-a5d2-431f-84b7-fe038e8bd7a2.titulo: Busking: operating on the fly and reading the music live
21733fd6-a5d2-431f-84b7-fe038e8bd7a2.conteudo_rico:
To get started: When the band changes the setlist mid-show, the cuelist freezes. The operator who knows how to busk shines.

Not every show is pre-programmed. In a lot of live bands the operator plays the light like an instrument, reacting to the music in real time — that is busking. This lesson teaches how to prepare the console for improvisation (groups, palettes, effects and blinders on buttons), how to sync with the rhythm (tap tempo/BPM), and the mental structure of looks that lets you react to chorus, drop, ballad and silence without freezing up.

  • What busking is: Busking is operating live, building looks on the spot in response to the music, without a locked cuelist. It requires a console very well prepared with groups, palettes and effects ready on buttons, and an operator with a musical ear and reflexes. It is the dominant mode in band shows with a flexible setlist.
  • Prepare the console for improvisation: Before the show, keep at hand: groups by position/type, a color palette, key positions, and buttons dedicated to strobe, blinder/bump, chases and blackout. The less you have to think about where each thing is, the more you react to the music. Labeling everything is essential.
  • Sync with the rhythm (tap tempo / BPM): Effects and chases have a speed you can 'tap' to the beat of the music via tap tempo (you tap the button on the beat and the effect takes the BPM). Syncing strobes and movements to the beat is what makes the light 'dance' along with the band.
  • Structure of looks by musical moment: Have mental looks for each situation: verse (soft, base color, little movement), chorus (more intensity and color, medium movement), drop/climax (strobe, blinder, beams in the air), ballad (warm, low, static), break/silence (blackout or a single spot). Switching looks at the right transition is the soul of busking.
  • Build and tear down looks live: The technique is to add layers: a color base (washes) + position (movings) + effect (chase/movement) + seasoning (strobe/blinder at the peaks). At the breathing-room moments, tear down layers (remove the effect, lower the intensity). Thinking in layers avoids the chaos of fiddling with fixture by fixture in the heat of the show.
  • Common mistakes and discipline: Too much strobe tires and risks the audience's health; movement all the time becomes visual pollution; a blinder held too long dazzles. The good operator's discipline is to save the strong effects for the peaks and respect the breathing room — contrast is what gives impact.

Equipment / details

  • Console with tap tempo and effect buttons: Consoles with tap tempo let you match chases and effects to the BPM by tapping the beat. Dedicated buttons (strobe, bump, blackout) are the interface of busking. Avolites and ChamSys are especially celebrated for this use.
  • Submaster faders / per-group intensity: Faders that control the intensity of whole groups (front, rear, washes) let you mix the light live like a mixer — bring up the chorus, lower the verse — without touching each fixture.

Step by step

  1. Prepare the console for busking: 1) Create groups by position and type. 2) Build the color palette and the key positions. 3) Put strobe, blinder/bump, chases and blackout on labeled buttons. 4) Map submaster faders per group. 5) Do a 'dry run' building and tearing down looks.
  2. Match the effect to the beat: 1) Start a chase/effect on a group. 2) Use tap tempo by tapping the beat of the music. 3) Fine-tune the speed. 4) Combine with strobe on the accents. The movement now 'breathes' with the band.
  3. Operate by musical moment: Verse: base color + little movement. Pre-chorus: bring up the intensity. Chorus/drop: add an effect, beams in the air and a bump of blinder/strobe on the accents. Break: tear everything down to a spot or a blackout. Start building again on the next part.
  4. Tear down elegantly: At the end of a song or in a pause, lower the submasters, remove the effects and take it to a smooth blackout or a single focus. Avoid abrupt cuts unless the music calls for it.

In practice: With a band that changed the setlist at the last minute, the cuelist would have been useless. The operator was ready to busk: in the verse, amber washes and slight movement of the spots; in the chorus, he brought up the submasters, threw the beams into a fan matched by tap tempo to the beat and gave blinder bumps on the accents; in the drop, he let loose strobe in short bursts; in the melodic bridge, he tore everything down to a single warm spot on the lead singer. The light followed the band beat by beat.

Summary

  • Busking is playing the light like an instrument, reacting to the music in real time.
  • The console must be prepared: groups, palettes, positions and effects on buttons at hand.
  • Think in layers and by musical moment; save the strong effects for the peaks.

Busking: operating on the fly

Not every show has a ready-made timeline. In busking you operate live, reading the music and triggering looks on the spot — it is the standard at concerts, parties and DJ sets. The key is to read the energy of the music and match it with the light: in the intro/verse, hold back (master low, calm color, no effect); in the build, grow; in the DROP/chorus, throw everything in (master maxed out, movement, strobe, blinder into the audience). Prepare playbacks and banks beforehand (colors, chases, effects) and use bumps (momentary buttons) on the beat. Reading the beat and anticipating the turnaround is what sets the good operator apart.

Practice live in the simulator below: follow the sections and match the energy.

9262b37f-9467-42e6-92c7-8f09baa7bd22.titulo: Simulator 1 — Build a basic rig and address a moving head
9262b37f-9467-42e6-92c7-8f09baa7bd22.conteudo_rico:
To get started: Time to get hands-on. We'll build a small rig from scratch and bring the first moving head to life.

This is the course's first 'simulator': a complete step-by-step, from paper to lit fixture. You will plan a basic rig, build the power and DMX chain, physically address a moving head, patch it on the console and verify. It is the applied synthesis of Modules 2 and 3. The exact menu names change per console — follow the flow and consult your model's manual.

  • The example rig: We'll build: 4 LED PARs (8ch each), 2 moving spots (16ch each) and 1 hazer (1ch). All in one universe. Total channels: 4×8 + 2×16 + 1 = 32 + 32 + 1 = 65 channels — comfortably within a universe's 512.
  • Plan before touching a cable: Draw a mini-plot: where each fixture goes, where the DMX runs (daisy chain) and the AC. Build the patch table with the rule 'next = current + channels'. That 10-minute planning saves an hour of confusion during setup.
  • Power chain: LED PARs and movings on constant power (non-dim); add up the watts and check the phase/breaker. The hazer also on its own AC. Use proper cables and plugs, with a service loop at the connectors. Never plug an LED into a dimmer circuit.
  • Data chain: From the console, a DMX cable enters the first PAR; PAR 1 OUT → PAR 2 IN... up to the last moving; on the last fixture's DMX OUT, the 120 Ω terminator. Data cable, not audio.
  • Address the moving head: On the moving spot's display, enter the address menu (Address/DMX Address), and set the mode (16ch) and the start address as per the table. Confirm that the mode matches what will be set in the patch.
  • Verify before programming: After patching (the next part of the step-by-step), select each fixture on the console and open the dimmer/color to confirm that the correct physical light responds. Only then move on to groups, palettes and scenes.

Equipment / details

  • 4× LED PAR (8ch): The rig's color base. Addresses calculated in the table. Connected to constant power and in a DMX daisy chain.
  • 2× Moving Spot (16ch): Mobile patterns, position and color. Addressed on the display and patched in an identical 16ch mode on the console.
  • 1× Hazer (1ch): Creates the mist that makes the beams visible. One DMX channel controls the density. Its own AC and ventilation.
  • DMX terminator, DMX and AC cables, splitter (optional): Data and power cables kept separate; a terminator on the last fixture; a splitter if the line grows.

Step by step

  1. 1. Build the patch table: PAR1=1–8, PAR2=9–16, PAR3=17–24, PAR4=25–32; Spot1=33–48, Spot2=49–64; Hazer=65. Note the model, mode, universe and address of each fixture.
  2. 2. Power up safely: Add up the watts, check the phase and the breaker, connect the fixtures to constant power. Let the movings do their reset/homing.
  3. 3. Run the DMX in a daisy chain: Console → PAR1 IN; PAR1 OUT → PAR2 IN; ... → Spot2 IN; Spot2 OUT → Hazer IN; Hazer OUT → 120 Ω terminator.
  4. 4. Address each fixture on the display: Set the address and mode per the table (PAR1 at 1/8ch; ...; Spot1 at 33/16ch; Spot2 at 49/16ch; Hazer at 65/1ch).
  5. 5. Patch on the console: Select each model/mode in the library and enter the identical universe and address. Assign logical fixture numbers (1 to 7).
  6. 6. Verify fixture by fixture: Select each one and open the dimmer/color. If the wrong light comes on, fix the address or mode before continuing.

In practice: An operator built exactly this rig for a small event. It took 10 minutes on the patch table, he powered up checking the phase, ran the DMX with a terminator, addressed on the display and patched matching the modes. During verification, Spot2 lit up together with Spot1: both were at address 33. He adjusted Spot2 to 49 on the display, tested again — perfect — and moved on to creating groups and scenes.

Summary

  • Planning the plot and the patch table beforehand saves time and prevents errors.
  • Power (constant power for LED) and data (daisy chain + terminator) are separate chains.
  • Address on the display and patch with an identical mode, then verify fixture by fixture.

Simulator 1 — step by step

This is the first hands-on exercise: build a rig and address it. Add the fixtures to the truss (moving heads, LED PAR, wash), remembering that each type takes up a number of channels (mode). Then address each one: the first on channel 1, the next right after the end of the previous one (a 16-channel moving at 1 takes up 1–16, so the next starts at 17). Auto-patch does this in sequence for you. The goal appears in the checklist: 4 or more fixtures, all addressed, without overlap and within the 512-channel universe.

Complete the goal in the simulator below.

6ae1bd5f-b875-4960-bad9-bb8167330217.titulo: Simulator 2 — Create groups, palettes and a complete scene
6ae1bd5f-b875-4960-bad9-bb8167330217.conteudo_rico:
To begin: With the rig alive, let's turn loose fixtures into tools: groups, palettes and your first recorded look.

Second simulator: starting from the rig you already patched and verified, you will create logical groups, build color and position palettes, and record your first complete scene with timing. It is the hands-on application of Module 4. Remember: the buttons change names by console (Record, Store, Update...), but the flow select → adjust → record is universal.

  • Define the rig's groups: For the example rig, create: 'PARs' (all 4), 'Odd PARs' and 'Even PARs' (for chases), and 'Spots' (the 2 movers). Groups by type and by odd/even give immediate flexibility.
  • Build the color palette: Create at least 4 colors on the PARs and spots: warm amber, deep blue, magenta and white. Record each one as a color palette. Standardizing the palette now makes every future scene consistent.
  • Build the position palettes: On the spots, create key positions: 'Center' (aiming at the middle of the stage), 'Audience' (pointing up/forward) and 'Floor' (gobo on the floor). Use only pan/tilt to keep them reusable.
