  • Record the first scene: Build a look: PARs in warm amber at 70%, spots in white aiming at 'Center' with a soft gobo. Record it as a scene/cue with a 3 s fade. This is your first 'photograph of light'.
  • Use the live reference: Since the scene points to the palettes, if you later readjust the 'Center' palette (the stage changed), the scene stays correct. Test it: change the position in the palette slightly and watch the scene follow.
  • Clear and test: Always do a 'clear' on the active programming before testing, so you don't record 'garbage'. Play the scene from scratch and confirm that the look and the fade come out as expected.

Equipment / details

  • Console (or PC software) with group, palette and playback areas: Use MagicQ, Onyx or grandMA3 onPC if you are practicing without hardware. The group, color/position palette and record areas exist in all of them, under their own names.
  • 3D visualizer (Capture, MagicVis, grandMA3 3D): A tool that simulates the rig on screen, letting you program groups, palettes and scenes without the physical fixtures. Excellent for training this simulator at home.

Step by step

  1. 1. Create the groups: Select the 4 PARs → Record Group → a free button → name it 'PARs'. Repeat for 'Odd PARs' (1 and 3), 'Even PARs' (2 and 4) and 'Spots' (the 2 movers).
  2. 2. Create the color palettes: Select 'PARs' + 'Spots', set warm amber → Record in the color area → name it. Repeat for blue, magenta and white.
  3. 3. Create the position palettes: Select 'Spots', aim at the center → Record as a position palette → 'Center'. Repeat for 'Audience' and 'Floor' (pan/tilt only).
  4. 4. Build and record the scene: Call 'PARs' + amber palette at 70%; 'Spots' + white + 'Center' + soft gobo. Record as cue 1 with a 3 s fade. Name it 'Opening'.
  5. 5. Clear and validate: Do a Clear, play back the 'Opening' scene and check the look and the timing. Adjust and re-record (Update) if needed.

In practice: On top of the Simulator 1 rig, the operator created groups ('PARs', 'Even', 'Odd', 'Spots'), a 4-color palette and 3 positions for the spots. They recorded the 'Opening' scene (amber PARs at 70% + white spots at center). When they readjusted the 'Center' position because the stage was deeper, the 'Opening' scene updated by itself — exactly the power of the live reference of palettes.

Summary

  • Groups and palettes turn loose fixtures into fast operating tools.
  • The first scene combines group + color palette + position + intensity + fade.
  • Palettes as a live reference make scenes adapt when the stage changes.

Simulator 2 — create groups, palettes and a scene

Now that the rig is addressed, build a complete scene on the console interface: (1) select fixtures and create Groups (movers, odd/even); (2) apply color palettes (Colour Presets) and adjust position on the encoders; (3) give intensity (At Full/Dimmer); (4) do a Store on an executor to save the scene and raise the fader to play it back. This is the same flow as the grandMA — practice until it feels natural.

Build the scene in the trainer below.

8896a57e-2a38-4b61-862f-a104b4ba1325.titulo: Simulator 3 — Build a cuelist for a song
8896a57e-2a38-4b61-862f-a104b4ba1325.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The exercise that brings it all together: take a real song and turn its structure into a complete cuelist.

Final simulator: you will analyze the structure of a song (intro, verse, chorus, bridge, final chorus, ending), create a look for each part and chain them into a cuelist with timing. It is the integration of the whole course — patch, groups, palettes, scenes, cuelist and the musical sensibility of busking, now structured. At the end, you will have a runnable 'mini-show' triggered with GO.

  • Analyze the musical structure: Listen to the song and mark the sections: Intro (atmosphere), Verse (intimate), Pre-chorus (build-up), Chorus (maximum energy), Bridge/break (breather), Final chorus (climax) and Ending (resolution). Each section becomes a cue. Note the approximate timings or the cues.
  • Define a look per section: Intro: spots in blue, low, haze; Verse: soft amber PARs + 1 spot on the vocalist; Pre-chorus: raise intensity, more saturated color; Chorus: everything open, movement, beams/chase; Bridge: drop down to a single warm focus; Final chorus: climax with strobe/blinder on the accents; Ending: slow fade to blackout.
  • Record the cues with timing: Record each look as a cue in the same cuelist, in order, with coherent fades: the intro comes in over 4 s, the verse transitions over 2 s, the chorus can 'burst' in 0.5 s, the ending goes out over 6 s. Timing is what gives the transition its musical feel.
  • Chain and rehearse the cuelist: Run the cuelist from cue 1 to the last one with GO, ideally with the song playing, marking where to press GO on each turn. Adjust fades that came out too slow or too fast. Repeat until the transitions land on the beat of the music.
  • Combine cuelist with live effects: Leave the 'skeleton' in the cuelist (structure and colors) and keep strobe, blinder and bumps on buttons to season live on the accents. This way you get the safety of the cuelist and the expressiveness of busking at the same time.
  • Document and repeat: Note the order of the cues and the cues themselves (e.g. 'GO at the start of the chorus'). Save the show. A documented cuelist can be repeated night after night or handed to another operator — a principle that comes straight from the show's script/rider.

Equipment / details

  • Console with cuelist on a playback and GO button: The song's cuelist goes on a playback; the operator advances with GO on the turns. Extra buttons hold strobe/blinder for the accents. It is the classic setlist-show setup.
  • Audio player / reference metronome: Playing the song during rehearsal (or using the BPM as a reference) is essential to match the fade times and mark the GO cues precisely.

Step by step

  1. 1. Map the sections: Listen to the song and write the order: Intro, Verse, Pre-chorus, Chorus, Bridge, Final Chorus, Ending. Mark the timings/cues of each turn.
  2. 2. Create the look of each section: For each part, build the look with groups + palettes (color, position, intensity, effect) according to the energy of the section.
  3. 3. Record the cuelist: Record cue 1 (Intro) with its fade; repeat for each section in order in the same cuelist, adjusting fade/delay according to the musical transition.
  4. 4. Rehearse with the song: Play the song and run the cuelist with GO, marking the exact point of each GO. Correct timings until it lands right.
  5. 5. Add live seasoning: Keep strobe/blinder/bump on buttons for the accents of the chorus and the climax, fired manually over the cuelist.
  6. 6. Save and document: Note the cues ('GO on the drop'), save the show and make a backup of the console file.

In practice: For a ~3 min pop song, the operator mapped 7 sections and created 7 cues: blue intro with haze; amber verse with focus on the vocalist; pre-chorus raising the color; chorus with fanned beams and a chase on the PARs; bridge on a single warm spot; final chorus with strobe on the accents; ending in a slow fade to blackout over 6 s. They rehearsed with the track playing, adjusted the fades, and marked on the script where to press GO. In the show, they ran the cuelist and still gave manual blinder bumps on the accents — structure + expression.

Summary

  • The structure of the song becomes the structure of the cuelist: each section is a cue.
  • Fade times give the transitions their musicality — a burst on the chorus, a slow fade on the ending.
  • Combine the safety of the cuelist with the live seasoning of busking and document everything.

Simulator 3 — build a cuelist for a song

With the scenes ready, chain them into a cuelist that tells the song: a cue for the intro, another for the chorus, one for the solo, the blackout at the end — each with the right fade time (snappy on the turns, smooth on the passages). Run it with GO and adjust the times. A well-built cuelist makes the whole show repeatable with one finger.

Build and run the cuelist in the simulator below.

e12c1798-ed6e-4649-9eef-5a0aab742f51.titulo: Briefing, lighting design and the technical rider
e12c1798-ed6e-4649-9eef-5a0aab742f51.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Every great show starts on a document. Knowing how to design and communicate on paper is what separates the amateur from the professional.

Before the setup comes the design. This lesson walks through the planning cycle: the briefing (understanding goal, space, style and budget), the development of the lighting design/plan, and the key piece of technical communication — the technical rider — which tells the venue exactly which fixtures, power, console and structures the show needs. It is the knowledge that turns an idea into something executable and contractable.

  • The briefing: A meeting/document that defines the foundation of the project: objectives and vision, space and layout, desired style and atmosphere, colors and temperature, functionality (what to light), control/programming, budget, deadlines, crew and the venue's technical requirements. A good briefing aligns client, artist and crew and avoids costly misunderstandings.
  • Developing the design: With the briefing, the LD defines the concept (palette, angles, moments), selects equipment by need and by specs, does basic lighting calculations (coverage, intensity, distance) and positions the fixtures. They consider available power, control (console/software) and safety. The result is the lighting plot and the cue/scene list.
  • The design script: A document that organizes everything into stages: introduction, objectives, site survey, crew, conceptual design, lighting plot, equipment selection, power plan, programming, budget, schedule, tests, safety, approval and presentation. Adaptable to the complexity of each event.
  • The lighting technical rider: A document handed to the venue/production with ALL the requirements: event data and schedule (setup, soundcheck, show), fixture list (model and quantity: washes, spots, PARs, strobes, moving heads, effects), positioning, colors/filters, console and software, DMX cables, power requirements (total power and points), structures (grid, truss, supports) and required crew. It is the technical contract of the show.
  • Power, structure and personnel in the rider: The rider specifies the estimated total power and the power points (for the venue to size), the support structures (lighting grid, ground supports) and the local crew needed for setup/operation. The more precise it is, the fewer surprises on the day.
  • Licenses and responsibility: Events require a permit, safety report, Fire Department authorization and, for electrical installations, an engineer's ART. The lighting design is part of the documentation that supports these licenses. Working legally protects the professional and the public.

Equipment / details

  • Plot/lighting plan software: Tools like Vectorworks/Spotlight, WYSIWYG, Capture or MagicQ/grandMA 3D help draw the plan, position fixtures and preview the design before the setup. They generate the visual documentation of the rider.
  • Technical rider template: A template with fields for event, schedule, fixture list, console, DMX, power, structures and crew. Standardizing the rider speeds up quoting and communication with any venue.

Step by step

  1. Conduct a briefing: Ask: what is the goal and the atmosphere? What is the space like (dimensions, ceiling height, power points)? What is the style/palette? Is there a camera? What is the budget and the deadline? Who is the crew? Document the answers — they guide the whole design.
  2. Sketch the design: Define the concept and the palette; select fixtures by function (color wash, pattern spots, aerial beams, blinders); position them on the plot considering angles and distance; estimate the power (sum of watts) and choose the console.
  3. Write the technical rider: Fill in: event data/schedule; fixture list with model and quantity; positioning; colors/filters; console and software; quantity/length of DMX cables; total power and power points; structures; required crew. Attach the plot. Sign and date.
  4. Validate with the venue: Send the rider in advance and confirm with the venue's technical crew that the power, the structures and the points exist. Adjust whatever the venue cannot provide before the setup day.

In practice: For a tour show, the LD did the briefing with the production (12×8 m stage, 7 m ceiling height, warm palette, with cameras for the video wall), drew the plot in software, selected the rig (washes, spots, beams and blinders) and wrote the rider: fixture list and quantities, grandMA3 console, estimated total power and points, front and back truss, and 4 local technicians. They sent it to the venue a week ahead; the venue confirmed everything except one power point, which was arranged in time. On the day, the setup flowed without surprises.

Summary

  • The briefing defines goal, space, style, budget and deadline — the foundation of everything.
  • The design becomes a plot and a cue list; the technical rider is the technical contract sent to the venue.
  • Power, structures, crew and licenses must be foreseen and validated before the setup.

From briefing to technical rider

Everything starts with the briefing: understanding the event, the artist, the space and the budget. From that comes the lighting design (concept + plot) and the technical rider — the document that the production sends to the venue/contractor stating exactly what the show needs to happen the same way anywhere. A good lighting rider brings: equipment list (fixtures and quantities), power requirements (kVA, phases, grounding), rigging/structure points (truss, motors, WLL), stage plot (stage map and positions), patch/addressing, haze/effects needs, schedule and contacts. Whatever belongs to the dressing room, hotel or setlist goes into the hospitality/production rider — not the technical one.

Build the rider in the interactive below.

eff6491c-7a57-410b-9e31-905a29ec1b10.titulo: Crew, communication, jargon and the career path
eff6491c-7a57-410b-9e31-905a29ec1b10.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Light is teamwork. Speaking the language of the trade and knowing how to grow in your career is as technical as addressing a DMX.

This final lesson closes the course from the human and professional side: how a lighting crew is organized, the importance of communication over intercom with sound, stage and direction, the technical vocabulary (jargon) you need to master to be understood, and the path of growth in the career — from assistant to light designer. It is what turns technical knowledge into a sustainable trajectory.

  • The lighting crew: Typical roles: Light Designer (concept), operator/programmer (console), lighting technicians (setup, focus, maintenance), riggers (structure/height) and crew chief. Define roles and responsibilities before the event. In small crews, functions overlap; on big tours, each role is specialized.
  • Communication at the event: Light dialogues all the time with sound (PA), video/LED, stage manager and direction. Intercom (headset communication) is the central tool: scene cues, adjustments, emergencies. Clear and objective communication avoids errors and speeds up solving problems live.
  • Essential jargon (part 1): Focus (aiming the fixture at the right point); Patch (DMX addressing); Cue (a triggerable state of light); Cue List (a sequence of cues); Dimmer (intensity control); Gobo (a plate that projects patterns); Fader (a sliding intensity control); Blackout (turning everything off); Crossfade (a smooth transition between looks).
  • Essential jargon (part 2): Strobe (intermittent light); Fixture (a single lighting unit); Wash (broad, soft light); Color Wheel (color wheel); LX (abbreviation for lighting); Bump (a momentary increase in intensity); Top Hat (an accessory that controls the spread of the light); Spill (unwanted light that leaks where it shouldn't). Mastering this vocabulary is speaking the language of the trade.
  • Documentation and organization: Saving and organizing show files (console backups), plot, rider and script ensures repeatability and professionalism. A well-documented show can be re-set up by any technician — and it protects you if something goes wrong. Version and name the files clearly.
  • The career path: Start as an assistant/intern alongside experienced professionals; get real stage hours; learn multiple consoles (train on the free software); take care of safety and certifications (NR 35); keep up with trends and technology; build a portfolio and reputation. Continuous practice and a network of contacts are what sustain the career.

Equipment / details

  • Intercom system: Headsets with communication between FOH (lighting/sound), stage and direction. Essential for cues, adjustments and live emergencies. Knowing how to operate the intercom concisely is part of professionalism.
  • Show storage and backup: USB drive/disk and cloud to keep console files, plot, rider and script organized and versioned. Ensures you can repeat the show and transfer it to another operator.

Step by step

  1. Organize the crew for an event: List the roles needed (LD, operator, technicians, rigger), assign responsibilities in writing, define who speaks on the intercom and set the schedule (setup, tests, rehearsal, show, teardown).
  2. Communicate a cue clearly: On the intercom, be objective: 'Light, standby cue 12... GO'. Confirm receipt. Standardize 'standby' (prepare) and 'GO' (execute) to avoid ambiguity between light, sound and stage.
  3. Build your portfolio: Record (with permission) photos/videos of your shows, keep the riders and plots you made, and document the equipment you master. Keep material that proves your experience for future contracts.
  4. Growth plan: Train on the free software (MagicQ, Onyx, grandMA3 onPC); get the safety certifications; seek assistant roles on real productions; and keep learning — the industry changes fast.

In practice: In a theater, the cue worked over the intercom: the stage manager gave 'standby LX 12' and, at the exact moment of the scene, 'LX 12 GO' — the operator executed the cue at the right time. The crew (LD, operator, two technicians and a rigger) had roles defined in writing. The show, fully documented (console file, plot and script versioned), was re-set up in another city by a substitute operator without losing quality — proof of the value of communication and documentation.

Summary

  • Lighting is teamwork: clear roles and objective communication over intercom avoid errors.
  • Mastering the trade's jargon is a prerequisite to being understood and hired.
  • A career is built with real practice, safety, mastery of several consoles and professional documentation.

The crew's language and the career path

On stage, communication is fast, direct and full of slang — many orders are shouted amid the noise. Understanding the jargon (bring up the general, kill, back light, gel, follow spot, focus, transition, workaround) is part of the craft and of safety. Working well as a team, communicating clearly and respecting the setup hierarchy opens doors. The career path usually is: start in setup/roadie, become a rigger/electrician, then an operator, and with experience and a portfolio, a programmer and Light Designer. Courses, home practice (onPC), networking and a good portfolio speed up this growth.

Test the jargon in the interactive below.

0ced66e6-4955-41cd-b0e5-e018fcd8c4e2.titulo: Consoles and Lighting Desks: the brain of the show
0ced66e6-4955-41cd-b0e5-e018fcd8c4e2.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Before any fixture lights up, someone has to tell it what to do. That someone is the console. In this lesson you meet the desks that command the biggest stages in the world and understand the common logic behind all of them.

The console (or lighting desk) is the computer that generates and sends the DMX/sACN signals to all the fixtures. Mastering the console is mastering the show: it is where you do the patch (register the fixtures), program the scenes and fire everything live. We'll go from the patch/fixture concept to executors and cuelists, passing through the market's reference platforms.

  • What a console really does: A console doesn't 'have' any light itself. It calculates channel values (intensity, pan, tilt, color, gobo...) and transmits them over protocols like DMX512 (up to 512 channels per universe) and, over a network, Art-Net and sACN (hundreds of universes). The intelligence is in how it organizes those channels into objects called fixtures.
  • Patch: registering the fixtures: Patch is telling the console WHICH fixture is at WHICH DMX address, using WHICH personality (the manufacturer's profile/library). You choose the model and the mode (e.g. 16-bit, extended mode), define the universe and the start address, and the console then controls that fixture by attributes (Dimmer, Color, Gobo) instead of raw channel numbers.
  • Programming: from preset to cue: You adjust the fixtures (manually or via color/position/beam presets), capture that state in a cue (scene) and organize cues in sequence within a cuelist (sequence). Each cue carries fade-in/fade-out times and delays. Presets and palettes make everything reusable: change the 'Blue' palette and every cue that uses it updates.
  • Firing live: executors and playbacks: Executors (grandMA), playbacks (ChamSys/Avolites) or faders/buttons (ETC) are the physical 'triggers'. You assign cuelists, chasers and effects to faders and buttons and fire them at the right moment, on their own or following timecode/music. It is the interface between the programming and the performance.

Equipment / details

  • MA Lighting grandMA2 (full-size / light / onPC): The worldwide reference console on big tours and TV. It works by executors (physical faders and buttons), with up to thousands of channels via nodes/NPU. Strong command-line syntax (Channel/Fixture, At, Thru, Store). grandMA2 is still a market standard for reliability; its successor is the grandMA3, which runs on MA3 hardware and also in MA2 mode.
  • MA Lighting grandMA3 (full-size / light / compact / onPC): MA's current platform. Completely new engine, multi-instance fixtures, phasers (phase-based effects), high-resolution encoders and a wide touch screen. Used at festivals and big shows. Steeper learning curve, but it's the way of the future; it runs the free grandMA3 onPC software for study (with limited output without a dongle/node).
  • ChamSys MagicQ (MQ80, MQ250M Stadium, PC/Mac): Strong in value for money and in native media/pixel mapping. MagicQ onPC is free and complete for programming (up to 4 universes without hardware; more with a license/nodes). It works with playbacks, and has effect tools and an internal media server. Widely used by independent LDs, churches and mid-size producers.
  • Avolites Titan (Quartz, Tiger Touch II, Arena, Sapphire Touch): The Titan operating system, known for the speed of live programming (busking) and for the Pixel Mapper. Fixtures on handles, key-frame shapes (effects) very intuitive. Popular in shows with lots of improvisation and on electronic music stages. Titan Go/Titan Simulator for study.
  • Obsidian Control Onyx (NX1, NX2, NX Wing, PC): Successor to the Martin M-PC/M-Series. Onyx is free on PC (unlocks 1 full universe; more with a key/hardware). Modern interface, good effects engine and integration with fixtures. A good low-cost professional entry point.
  • ETC Eos Family (Gio @5, Ion Xe, Element 2, Nomad/ETCnomad): The absolute standard in theater and on Broadway. 'Tracking' philosophy (a value stays until it is changed) and magic sheets. Strong in controlling ellipsoidals and conventional dimmers, integrates perfectly with ETC Sensor dimmers. ETCnomad runs the Eos software on PC. Eos is the reference for theater; MA/Chamsys/Avo dominate music shows.

Step by step

  1. 1. Define the rig: List all the fixtures (moving heads, PARs, strobes) and the universe/address of each one, avoiding channel overlap.
  2. 2. Do the patch: On the console, select model + mode (correct personality), assign a fixture number, universe and start address matching what is set on the fixture.
  3. 3. Create palettes/presets: Program color, position (focus) and beam palettes for quick reuse.
  4. 4. Program cues: Set the scene, define fade times and store it in a cuelist in the show's order.
  5. 5. Assign executors/playbacks: Put cuelists, chasers and effects on faders/buttons for live firing.
  6. 6. Test GO and timing: Run the cuelist from start to finish checking transitions, blackout and the emergency cue.

In practice: At a festival, the resident LD usually runs grandMA2/MA3 or MagicQ. The fixture that comes up 'wrong' on screen is almost always a crooked patch: the fixture's DMX mode differs from the personality chosen on the console. Before blaming the cable, check the mode and the address — it is the number 1 cause of a moving head that 'won't move'.

Summary

  • The console generates and sends DMX/sACN; the patch links each fixture to an address and a profile.
  • Programming becomes cues and cuelists; live firing happens through executors/playbacks.
  • grandMA2/MA3 and MagicQ/Avolites/Onyx dominate shows; ETC Eos is the reference in theater.

The Avolites Titan workflow

The Avolites (Titan) has a slightly different logic from the MA. You select the fixtures (or a Group) and use Locate to bring them to a known state (open, white, centered focus, at full) — a great starting point. Then you choose the attribute bank (Intensity, Position, Colour, Beam) and adjust with the wheels, or apply ready-made palettes (groups, colors, positions). When the look is ready, you press Record and touch a Playback to record it there; the playback fader plays the look and the button gives the Go. Key terms: Locate, Clear, Record, Palettes, Playbacks.

Train the Titan interface on the replica below. The real software (Titan PC/Simulator) is free on the Avolites site.

a65330d1-b068-48d2-886b-262f1d2df143.titulo: Moving Heads: Spot, Beam, Wash and Hybrids
a65330d1-b068-48d2-886b-262f1d2df143.conteudo_rico:
To begin: They rotate, open fans of light, project logos and cut the air with thin beams. The moving head is the mobile star of the modern stage. In this lesson you learn to tell each type apart and to recognize the models that appear on riders all over the world.

A moving head is a motorized fixture with pan (horizontal rotation) and tilt (vertical rotation) controlled by DMX. The big division is by optics/function: Spot (gobos and sharpness), Beam (thin, intense beam), Wash (broad color wash) and Hybrid (does more than one role). Understanding this avoids buying/renting the wrong fixture for the desired effect.

  • Spot: the projector: It has projection optics, gobo wheels (fixed and rotating), iris, focus and zoom. It serves to project textures, logos and defined designs on the floor, set or audience. Think of it as a mobile projector: sharpness is the keyword.
  • Beam: the beam: Very narrow beam optics (usually 1.5° to 4°) and very high apparent intensity. It doesn't 'wash' surfaces; it draws columns of light in the air (needs smoke/haze to appear). Great for 'pencil beams', aerial fans and impact effects.
  • Wash: the wash: Wash optics with wide zoom, color mixing (CMY or RGBW) and a soft beam. It covers large areas, does background colors, stage wash and cyclorama. It doesn't project sharp gobos; its strength is homogeneous color and coverage.
  • Hybrid: the wildcard: Combines two or three modes (Spot + Beam, or Spot + Beam + Wash) in a single body, switching optics/zoom. It reduces the number of fixtures and is ideal when there are few truss positions. In exchange, it is usually more expensive and no mode is as 'pure' as a dedicated one.
  • Light source and optics: Most modern ones are LED (long life, stable color) or discharge (Osram/Philips lamps like Sirius/MSD for maximum brightness). Common attributes: pan/tilt (8/16-bit), CMY or color wheel, gobos, prism, frost, focus, zoom, dimmer and shutter/strobe.

Equipment / details

  • Clay Paky Sharpy (Beam): The icon that popularized the modern beam. 189W discharge lamp, ~3° beam, extremely bright for its size. Used in columns of light and aerial fans. There are evolutions (Sharpy Plus with spot/wash). When a rider asks for a 'classic beam', it is often a Sharpy.
  • Clay Paky Mythos / Mythos 2 (Hybrid Spot+Beam): A reference hybrid: it switches between spot (with gobos and zoom) and narrow beam. ~470W discharge lamp. Widely used on TV and big shows for delivering two fixtures in one body.
  • Robe Pointe (Hybrid Beam/Spot/Wash): A compact and versatile hybrid that helped define the category: it does beam, spot with gobos/prisms and even a wash effect. Light, popular in rental. Also from Robe: MegaPointe and Forte (Robe iForte) — high-brightness discharge fixtures for big stages.
  • Robe MegaPointe / iForte / Esprite (Spot/high-brightness LED): MegaPointe is the hybrid evolution of the Pointe, very bright. iForte and Forte are very high-output LED for long distances. Esprite (LED spot) with a calibratable color engine (TE) widely used in TV/broadcast for its color fidelity.
  • Martin MAC (Aura, Quantum, Viper, Encore): Martin's (Harman) traditional line. MAC Aura (LED wash with an 'aura'/pixel-ring effect, a TV classic), MAC Quantum (spot/wash), MAC Viper (powerful discharge spot), MAC Encore (LED with cinematic color quality, Performance and Wash). A reference in color quality and build.
  • Chauvet Professional Maverick / Rogue (Spot/Beam/Wash LED): Chauvet's professional lines with great value for money. Maverick (e.g. MK Pyxis, Force series) and Rogue (e.g. R2/R3 Spot, Beam, Wash, and the R3X). Very present in mid-size rental, churches and regional tours.
  • Robe / Ayrton (Ayrton Khamsin, Diablo, Domino, Eurus series): Ayrton is a premium French brand of high-power LED and quality optics, strong in TV and big events (Khamsin, Domino, Diablo are spots; Eurus is a wash). Recognized for its brightness and optical engineering.
  • Elation (Proteus series, Fuze, Smarty): Elation has a strong IP65 (weatherproof) line with the Proteus series (Hybrid, Maximus, Excalibur), ideal for outdoor shows and festivals. Fuze (LED wash/spot) and Smarty Hybrid (compact). A good choice when there is a risk of rain.

Step by step

  1. 1. Define the function: Decide the effect: project a gobo (Spot), aerial beam (Beam), color wash (Wash) or all of it (Hybrid).
  2. 2. Check the brightness/distance: The greater the distance (high truss trim, big stage), the more power needed (discharge or high-output LED).
  3. 3. Choose the source: LED for stable color and long life; discharge for maximum apparent brightness in beams.
  4. 4. Patch in the right mode: Use the DMX mode (e.g. 16-bit/extended) compatible with what the show requires; check the number of channels.
  5. 5. Calibrate home/orientation: Do the reset (home) and check for inverted pan/tilt according to the hanging position.
  6. 6. Validate with haze: For beams/hybrids, you can only assess the beam with haze in the air.

In practice: A beginner confuses Beam with Wash and complains that 'the wash doesn't draw columns'. It doesn't, indeed — a wash bathes, a beam cuts. Rule of thumb: if you want to see the beam in the air, it's a Beam (and it needs haze); if you want to paint the wall/stage with color, it's a Wash; if you want a sharp logo/gobo on the floor, it's a Spot. For a stage with few truss positions, the Hybrid solves it, at the cost of budget.

Summary

  • Spot projects gobos, Beam draws beams, Wash bathes with color, Hybrid combines the roles.
  • Reference models: Sharpy/Mythos (Clay Paky), Pointe/MegaPointe/Forte (Robe), MAC (Martin), Maverick/Rogue (Chauvet), Ayrton and Elation Proteus.
  • The choice depends on function, distance/brightness, source (LED vs. discharge) and weather (IP).

Spot, Beam, Wash and the Hybrids

Reviewing the types: Spot (defined beam, gobo, zoom, prism), Beam (thin, intense column, aerial) and Wash (broad, soft color wash). The hybrids bring these worlds together in a single fixture: a Spot+Beam+Wash projects with a gobo, cuts the air with a columnar beam and also opens a color wash — versatile and therefore widely used on tour, where each mover has to do everything. The trade-off is that a hybrid may not be as extreme as a pure Beam or as soft as a dedicated Wash.

Compare Spot/Beam/Wash in the simulator below (the hybrid is the fixture that does all three).

8905d700-074e-4462-a61c-946246268da8.titulo: Fixed and LED Fixtures: PAR, Fresnel, Ellipsoidal and Pixel
8905d700-074e-4462-a61c-946246268da8.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Not everything needs to move. A good part of a stage's light comes from fixed, cheap and reliable fixtures. Knowing how to choose between PAR, Fresnel, ellipsoidal, pixel bar and blinder is what separates an amateur stage from a well-designed one.

Fixed fixtures have no pan/tilt: you aim them during setup and they stay. They are the base of scene light (key, fill, back) and of background effects. Today most are LED, but conventional ellipsoidals and Fresnels still reign in theater. Let's see what each optic does and when to use it.

  • PAR / PAR LED: The workhorse. A relatively fixed beam (defined by the lens/angle), lots of power per watt and low cost. In LED, it does RGBW/RGBA with color mixing by DMX. Used in stage color wash, back light and background lighting. The conventional PAR 64 is tradition; the PAR LED took over for its color and economy.
  • Fresnel: A Fresnel lens (concentric rings) gives a soft beam with diffuse edges, easy to blend between fixtures. It has a spot/flood adjustment (narrows/opens the beam). A classic of scene light in theater and TV for being controllable and 'sweet'. In LED, it keeps the quality with economy.
  • Ellipsoidal (ERS / Leko / Profile): The scalpel of light: a hard, shapeable beam. It has shutters to cut the beam into straight shapes, a gobo holder and an iris. Used to isolate an actor, project defined gobos and cut the set. Key brands: ETC Source Four (absolute reference) and LED versions (Source Four LED Series 3).
  • Batten / Pixel Bar / Batten: LED bars with individually addressable pixels, for chase effects, pixel mapping and back light with 'eyes'. Many have a warm white source + colored pixels. Used at the back of the stage, on risers and as a scenic element.
  • Blinder / Audience Light: High-output fixtures aimed at the audience, creating the 'blown out' effect on the choruses. Traditionally DWE lamps (2 or 4 'lamps'); today warm white LED versions. Little subtlety, lots of impact.

Equipment / details

  • ETC Source Four / Source Four LED Series 3 (Ellipsoidal): The worldwide ellipsoidal standard. The conventional version (HPL lamp) with interchangeable lenses (19°, 26°, 36°, 50°). The LED Series 3 brings arrays of up to 7 colors (Lustr) with excellent color quality and high CRI. Indispensable in theater and TV.
  • Chauvet Professional COLORado / Ovation (PAR/Fresnel/Ellipsoidal LED): Chauvet's pro line: COLORado (rugged PAR LED, many with IP) and Ovation (theater series: E-series ellipsoidal, F-series Fresnel) with a focus on color quality and quiet operation. Good for theaters and studios on a controlled budget.
  • ADJ (American DJ) — 5PX/7PX HEX, Vizi, Encore series (PAR LED and blinders): ADJ is strong in the entry/mid range: HEX PAR LEDs (6 colors, better white), affordable bars and blinders. Encore is the more professional line. Great value for churches, bars and beginning producers.
  • GLP (German Light Products) — impression X4 / JDC blinder-strobe: GLP is a premium reference. The impression X4 series (X4, X4 Bar, X4 atom) is a top-quality wash/pixel with zoom. The JDC1/JDC2 is a blinder + strobe LED hybrid widely used at festivals (RGB strobe strip + blinder tube).
  • SGM — P-series and Q-series (PAR/Wash LED IP65): SGM (Danish) is a specialist in rugged IP65 for permanent outdoor use and festivals. P-5/P-6 (powerful PAR/wash), Q-series. Famous for withstanding rain and sun and for high brightness.
  • Lit/legacy conventional — PAR 64, Fresnel 1kW/2kW, cyclorama: The conventionals still exist: PAR 64 (CP60/61/62 lamp), 1kW/2kW Fresnels and cyclorama fixtures (asymmetric flood) for uniform backgrounds. They require an external dimmer (they have no color electronics) and gels for color.

Step by step

  1. 1. Define the light's function: Key/fill (Fresnel), cut/gobo (ellipsoidal), color wash (PAR LED), effect/pixel (bar), audience impact (blinder).
  2. 2. Choose the angle: On ellipsoidals, select the lens (19°/26°/36°/50°) according to distance and target size.
  3. 3. Power vs. dimmer: A self-contained LED receives DMX directly; a conventional needs a dimmer channel.
  4. 4. Color: LED does color by DMX (RGBW/RGBA/HEX); a conventional uses a gel in the gel frame.
  5. 5. Cut/focus: On the ellipsoidal, adjust shutters, iris and focus; on the Fresnel, adjust spot/flood.
  6. 6. Mask spill: Use barndoors on the Fresnel/PAR to contain light where it shouldn't go.

In practice: Want to cut out exactly a frame or isolate a speaker? It's an ellipsoidal — only it has shutters for straight cuts. Want a soft wash that blends without seams? Fresnel. Want cheap color covering the stage? PAR LED. A common beginner error: trying to 'cut' with a PAR (impossible) or wanting to project a sharp gobo with a Fresnel (the edge is always diffuse).

Summary

  • Fixed fixtures are the cheap, reliable base: PAR (color), Fresnel (soft), ellipsoidal (cut/gobo), bar (pixel), blinder (impact).
  • The Source Four is the ellipsoidal standard; the PAR LED dominates color wash; GLP/SGM are premium.
  • The ellipsoidal cuts, the Fresnel blends; a conventional needs a dimmer and a gel.

PAR, Fresnel, Ellipsoidal and PC: which to use

The fixed fixtures differ by the edge of the beam and by the control. The PAR gives an oval, medium-edged beam, with no focus — the angle is defined by the lamp (CP60 wide, CP61 medium, CP62 narrow); it is cheap and powerful for wash and back light. The Fresnel has a very soft edge that blends with its neighbors and a spot↔flood focus (moving the lamp) — great for key light and fill. The Ellipsoidal (Leko/Source Four) has a hard, precise edge: the shutters frame/cut the beam and it accepts a gobo and iris — it is the theater's projector. The PC (plano-convex) sits in the middle: a semi-hard edge. Each one solves a problem.

Compare the four in the simulator below.

6e332f9e-d9fe-4de4-84bd-9ecabfdb4409.conteudo_rico:
To start: There are fixtures whose only job is to create impact: the flash that freezes time, the green beam that tears across the sky, the effect that makes the crowd scream. In this lesson you'll get to know strobes, lasers and special effects — and the safety precautions that come with them.

This category doesn't 'light the scene' in the classic sense; it creates moments. A strobe delivers extremely intense flashes; a laser draws beams and geometric shapes with coherent light; various effects (RGB strobe, blinder-strobe, 'eye' effects) create texture. Here, safety (epilepsy/photosensitivity and laser eye safety) is part of the technical knowledge.

  • Strobe: the flash: Fires very short pulses of highly intense light. You control rate, duration and, on RGB LED, color. Used in drops and impact moments. Watch out: flashes above ~3 Hz can trigger seizures in photosensitive people — warn the audience and use in moderation.
  • Laser: coherent light: Professional lasers (rated by color: red, green, blue, RGB combined into white) project ultra-thin beams and patterns via scanners (galvos) controlled by ILDA, plus DMX for triggering. They are classified (Class 3R/3B/4) and demand strict eye-safety precautions and, in many countries, a certified operator and controlled scan zones (regulated audience scanning).
  • Hybrid and texture effects: Blinder-strobe (e.g., GLP JDC1), RGB strobe bars, 'effect' fixtures with many mini-sources (eyes/pixels) and even moving heads with a strobe function. They fill the stage with movement and texture between scene moments.
  • Safety and responsibility: For strobe: communicate the presence of strobe lighting to the audience (a photosensitivity matter). For laser: never point at eyes, respect safety zones, don't improvise audience scanning, and follow local regulations. It's part of professional work, not optional.

Equipment / details

  • Martin Atomic 3000 (LED and DMX/strobe): The most iconic strobe in the world. Conventional (lamp) version and the Atomic 3000 LED, with extremely high output, an 'aura'/background color function and effects. Synonymous with professional strobe on tours and festivals.
  • GLP JDC1 / JDC2 (strobe + blinder hybrid): An RGB LED strobe tube combined with a warm-white blinder panel, with controllable pixels. Extremely popular as a 'dual' impact fixture on music stages.
  • Chauvet Professional Strike / Color STRIKE (blinder-strobe): The Strike line combines a warm-white blinder with strobe and colored pixels (Color STRIKE M is motorized). Good value for the effect of 'blasting' the crowd with color.
  • SGM Q-7 / X-5 (strobe/blinder IP65): SGM makes extremely high-power, IP65-rated strobes/washes for outdoor use. The Q-7 is a powerful wash-strobe; widely used at large outdoor festivals for its ruggedness and brightness.
  • Robe Spiider / Tarrantula (multi-source effect / wash-beam-strobe): Effect fixtures with a pixel flower (Spiider) and combined wash/beam/strobe (Tarrantula), creating rich textures and animated 'eyes'. Favorites of TV and touring for their visual versatility.
  • Kvant / Laserworld / Pangolin lasers (systems and control): Kvant (premium) and Laserworld (value) build RGB projectors of several watts; Pangolin (BEYOND/QuickShow) is the standard laser control and safety software/hardware (zones, scanning). Serious laser operation almost always goes through Pangolin.

Step by step

  1. 1. Define the moment: Strobe/effect for drops and impacts; laser for specific planned numbers.
  2. 2. Position safely: Lasers: map the scan zones, keep beams above the audience's heads if there's no approved scanning.
  3. 3. Set up control: Strobe via DMX (rate/duration/color); laser via ILDA + software (Pangolin) and/or DMX triggering.
  4. 4. Warn the audience: Signpost the use of strobe and laser lighting (signs/stage notice).
  5. 5. Moderate the rate: Avoid prolonged strobe frequencies that increase photosensitivity risk.
  6. 6. Test with someone responsible: For laser, test with a certified operator and an accessible e-stop.

In practice: The dangerous beginner mistake is treating a laser as 'just another fixture.' It isn't: it's a safety-class piece of equipment that can cause permanent eye injury. Audience scanning (sweeping the crowd) is regulated and requires calibrated power and measured zones — never improvise. As for strobe: gorgeous on the drop, but warn the audience and don't leave it flashing for minutes.

Summary

  • Strobe creates impact flashes; laser draws beams/shapes; hybrid effects add texture.
  • Key models: Martin Atomic, GLP JDC1, Chauvet Strike, Robe Spiider; Kvant/Laserworld lasers with Pangolin.
  • Safety is not optional: photosensitivity with strobe and eye safety/zones with laser.

Strobe and laser: effects with safety

The strobe gives rapid pulses of light that "freeze" motion — powerful in drops and turnarounds. But the 3 to 30 Hz range at high intensity can trigger seizures in photosensitive people: at an event it's mandatory to warn the audience and use it sparingly. The laser draws beams and patterns in the air (gorgeous with a hazer), but it's the most dangerous effect: aiming it at the audience can cause permanent eye injury. Golden rule: never sweep the audience — show lasers require a certified operator, a safety height above people's heads and a scan-fail system. Other effects: atomic/blinder, animated gobos and LED strobe.

Try it in the simulator below (strobe preview dimmed for safety).

f9779120-9290-44a4-9b6b-e8bce11a853c.titulo: Atmosphere Machines: the air that reveals the light
f9779120-9290-44a4-9b6b-e8bce11a853c.conteudo_rico:
To start: A gorgeous beam in the dark... that no one sees, because there's nothing in the air to reflect the beam. Atmosphere is the secret ingredient: without haze or fog, half your light effects simply disappear.

Atmosphere machines produce particles suspended in the air that make the light beam visible. There are different families — haze (a fine, long-lasting mist), fog/smoke (dense smoke), low fog (low-lying smoke that stays on the floor) — each with its own fluid. Understanding the difference avoids the classic mistake of 'flooding the stage with smoke' when what you wanted was a subtle haze.

  • Haze: the mist that makes beams appear: Produces a fine, even, long-lasting haze. It's what reveals beams and columns of light elegantly, without a 'cloud.' Water-based or oil-based (glycol) hazers. Oil haze tends to last longer in the air; water haze dissipates faster and leaves less residue. It's the standard for shows with moving heads/beams.
  • Fog / Smoke: dense smoke: Releases dense, visible clouds (a 'smoke burst' effect), great for one-off moments and entrances. It dissipates faster than haze and tends to 'rise.' Fog machines use a specific fluid; in excess, they can trigger fire alarms and cut visibility.
  • Low fog: floor-hugging smoke: Creates a carpet of smoke on the floor (a 'cloud at your feet' effect). Achieved by cooling the smoke (ice, CO2 or dedicated chiller units). Used in dramatic entrances, ballets and weddings. It doesn't rise because it's cooled/heavy smoke.
  • Fluids, safety and ventilation: Each machine requires the correct manufacturer fluid (water, glycol/oil). Venues need ventilation and attention to smoke/fire sensors. In TV studios, controlled haze and low-density fluids are preferred. Never mix incompatible fluids: it clogs and damages the machine.

Equipment / details

  • Antari (HZ-series hazers, F/Z-series fog, ICE low fog): A very widespread, affordable brand. HZ-400/HZ-500 and the Pro line of hazers (water and oil), F-series/Z-series fog machines and low fog (ICE). Good value for production companies, bands and churches; many with DMX and a timer.
  • Look Solutions (Unique, Viper, Tiny, Power Tiny): A premium German maker of hazers and fog. The Unique 2.1 is a world reference for top-quality haze (a system that keeps the fluid flowing without clogging). Viper and Tiny are very reliable compact fog/haze units. Standard in TV, theater and demanding tours.
  • MDG (Atmosphere / theONE / Me1): Canadian top-of-the-line. theONE and Atmosphere APS produce ultra-fine, stable haze using high-purity fluid and, in some models, CO2/N2 for maximum longevity and control. Standard in large productions, opera and top-tier TV.
  • Le Maitre / Smoke Factory / DF-50 (classic oil haze): The DF-50 (Diffusion) is a legendary oil hazer in film and TV for the atmospheric haze that 'paints' the air cinematically. Le Maitre and Smoke Factory are also traditional professional fog/haze brands.
  • Dedicated low fog generators (Antari ICE, MagicFX, CO2/dry ice): For the floor-hugging carpet: machines with a chiller, CO2 systems (jets/cryojets) and dry ice. MagicFX and Antari ICE are examples. They require planning (ice/CO2) and safety (asphyxiation/oxygen in enclosed spaces).

Step by step

  1. 1. Choose the effect: Haze to reveal beams throughout the show; fog for a one-off impact; low fog for a floor carpet.
  2. 2. Select the machine and fluid: Use the fluid recommended by the manufacturer for the correct family (water/oil).
  3. 3. Position and ventilate: Place the machine to distribute evenly; control air currents and fire sensors.
  4. 4. Control via DMX/timer: Set density (output) and intervals; haze usually at a low, continuous level.
  5. 5. Pre-fill the room: Build the haze before the show and keep a constant level, don't 'punch' smoke during it.
  6. 6. Assess with the lights on: You can only judge the right amount with the beams lit.

In practice: The secret to haze is consistency, not quantity: a low, steady level throughout the show reveals the beams without becoming a cloud. Beginners get it wrong two ways — they forget the atmosphere (and the beams vanish) or they overdo it (and the stage becomes a milky fog that kills the contrast). In venues with a fire detector, warn the production: haze can set off the alarm if the sensor isn't isolated/notified.

Summary

  • Without atmosphere, beams and columns of light don't appear; haze is the standard for revealing them.
  • Haze (fine, long-lasting mist), fog (dense/one-off) and low fog (floor-hugging) have distinct fluids and uses.
  • Key brands: Antari (affordable), Look Solutions and MDG (premium), DF-50 (film/TV).

Atmosphere: the air that reveals the light

Without particles in the air, the light beam is invisible — you only see where it lands. Atmosphere machines fill the air to reveal the beams. The hazer releases a fine, even mist that stays in the air a long time and reveals all beams equally — it's the show standard. The fog machine releases a dense cloud that dissipates fast (good for blasts and reveals). Low fog (dry ice/CO₂) stays on the floor (heavier than air), creating a carpet of cloud. Be careful with density: too little and the beams vanish; too much and you get a whiteout (the stage disappears, the audience is bothered and there's a risk of triggering the alarm). Ventilation and air conditioning disperse the mist — compensate with the output.

Find the sweet spot in the simulator below.

0b4b75a0-d01c-4bf2-bc39-c3074ed2a6ef.titulo: Distribution and Structure: DMX, dimmer, wireless, truss and hoist
0b4b75a0-d01c-4bf2-bc39-c3074ed2a6ef.conteudo_rico:
To start: The light fixtures are the stars, but nothing lights up or hangs without the infrastructure. Cables, splitters, dimmers, wireless DMX, truss and hoists are the skeleton and nervous system of the rig — and where most problems (and accidents) happen.

This lesson covers how the signal reaches each fixture (DMX distribution), how conventionals receive controlled power (dimmer), how to cut cables through the air (wireless DMX), and how everything hangs safely (truss and hoist). It's the foundation that supports — literally and electrically — everything else.

  • DMX chain and topology: DMX512 is serial and daisy-chained: it leaves the console, enters a fixture, exits (DMX Thru) to the next, up to 32 devices per line and a max of ~512 channels per universe. The line must be terminated with a 120-ohm terminator on the last fixture. The correct cable is DMX (110-120 ohm impedance), not microphone cable — using audio XLR causes errors.
  • DMX Splitter / Booster: When there are many fixtures or long cables, a splitter/booster receives one DMX signal and regenerates it into several isolated (opto-isolated) outputs, preventing flicker and protecting the console. It also splits the rig into independent branches (a short in one branch doesn't take down the others).
  • Dimmer and power: Conventional fixtures (PAR 64, Fresnel) have no intensity electronics: they need a dimmer that varies the electrical power according to DMX. Dimmer racks (e.g., ETC Sensor) control dozens of circuits. LED/moving fixtures are 'intelligent power' (they receive power directly and dim internally) — never connect an LED fixture to a dimmer channel.
  • Wireless DMX: Transmits DMX by radio, eliminating cables in mobile, outdoor or hard-to-route structures. Market standard: LumenRadio (CRMX), with a transmitter and receivers built into many fixtures. It works in 2.4 GHz with frequency hopping; it's stable, but requires RF planning in saturated environments.
  • Truss and rigging: Truss is the aluminum (lattice) structure that holds the fixtures. Types by section: square (4 chords) for load, triangular (3 chords) lighter. The build must respect load (SWL), rigging points and standards. Accessories: clamps, safety chain (mandatory) on each fixture.
  • Hoists and movement: Electric hoists (chain hoists) lift the truss to the trim. The CM Lodestar is a world reference. Common capacities: 1/4, 1/2, 1 and 2 tons. Controlled by a motor controller. Rigging is the highest-risk area — it requires a qualified rigger, load calculation and double safety.

Equipment / details

  • DMX splitters/boosters (ETC, Swisson, DMXKing, Botex): Opto-isolated distributors that regenerate the signal into several outputs. Swisson (XSR/XND) and ETC are professional references; DMXKing and Botex offer value. Essential in large rigs for stability and protection against cabling shorts.
  • Art-Net/sACN nodes (Luminex GigaCore/LumiNode, ELC, Pathway): They convert network (Art-Net/sACN) into physical DMX outputs. Luminex (GigaCore switches and LumiNode nodes) is a touring standard; Pathway and ELC too. They allow dozens of universes over a single network cable to the nodes, which then 'break out' into DMX.
  • Dimmer racks (ETC Sensor3 / Sensor+): The market standard for controlling conventionals: dimmer and/or relay modules, breaker protection, configurable curves. ETC Sensor is the reference in theater and studio. For LED/moving, direct power distribution (power distro) is used, not a dimmer.
  • LumenRadio CRMX (TX2 / various RX, TimoTwo built-in): The wireless DMX standard. Transmitters (e.g., TX2) and receivers; many fixtures ship with a TimoTwo module from the factory. Adaptive frequency hopping in 2.4 GHz, high stability. There's also W-DMX (Wireless Solution) as a professional alternative.
  • Global Truss (F31/F32/F33/F34) and Prolyte (H30V/S52/X30): Global Truss is very widespread and economical (F34 is the popular 290mm square). Prolyte (Belgian) is premium, with H/S/X series for different loads and spans. The choice depends on load (SWL), free span and connection compatibility (conical/bolted).
  • CM Lodestar / Columbus McKinnon (chain hoists) + controllers: The standard electric chain hoist of the events market. Models by capacity (1/4t to 2t) and speed. Operated by motor controllers (single/multi). They require inspection, a safety chain and operation by a qualified rigger. Alternatives: Liftket, ChainMaster.
  • Clamps, safeties and couplers (Doughty/Manfrotto, Riggatec): Rigging accessories: clamps that fasten the fixture to the truss, half-couplers, and the mandatory safety chain/cable (safety) as a second line. Doughty and Riggatec are common brands. No fixture goes up without a safety.

Step by step

  1. 1. Plan universes and branches: Divide the rig into logical DMX universes and branches; insert splitters where there are many fixtures/long cables.
  2. 2. Cable with correct DMX: Use DMX cable (110-120 ohm), daisy-chain connections and a 120-ohm terminator at the end of each line.
  3. 3. Separate power: Conventionals on the dimmer rack; LED/moving on direct power distro. Never LED on the dimmer.
  4. 4. Configure wireless when needed: Pair TX/RX CRMX, choose the universe and check the venue's RF.
  5. 5. Build the truss with calculated load: Respect the SWL, distribute the weight, use clamps and a safety on every fixture.
  6. 6. Fly with the hoist and lock the trim: Raise with chain hoists, check the level, lock the trim and provide double safety in the rigging.

In practice: Two mistakes that ruin shows and money: (1) using microphone XLR cable in place of DMX cable — it may 'work' at home and fail on stage with random flicker; and (2) connecting a moving head/LED to a dimmer channel — the fixture's electronics can't tolerate the dimmer's waveform and can be damaged. And the mistake that kills: a fixture without a safety chain. Safety is non-negotiable.

Summary

  • DMX is a serial chain with a terminator; splitters regenerate and isolate branches; nodes carry Art-Net/sACN to DMX.
  • Conventionals need a dimmer; LED/moving receive direct power; CRMX (LumenRadio) is the wireless standard.
  • Truss (Global/Prolyte) and hoists (CM Lodestar) support the rig — always with the SWL respected and a mandatory safety.

Structure and load: rigging with safety

The truss holds the fixtures, but it has a load limit. Each rigging point (motor/hoist) has a WLL (Working Load Limit — safe working load) and the truss has a total SWL. The load doesn't split equally among the motors: by beam statics, the closer a fixture is to a motor, the more weight goes onto it. Concentrating weight on one side can exceed the WLL of a motor even with the total within the SWL. Rules: distribute the load, stay within the WLL of each point and the SWL of the truss, add the weight of cables/multicables, and use a safety cable (secondary) on every fixture. Rigging is a regulated, life-safety matter — when in doubt, a responsible engineer/rigger.

b5bb5e2e-a736-4597-9e4a-f731f2829a5c.pergunta: Which professional is the author of the visual concept and produces the rider and the lighting plan?
b5bb5e2e-a736-4597-9e4a-f731f2829a5c.explicacao: The Light Designer conceives the design, defines the palette, angles, and key moments, and documents everything in the rider and the plot.

20a3900b-3b2d-4974-9f5c-9d129eb28387.pergunta: Operating 'on the fly,' reacting to the band in real time, is called:
20a3900b-3b2d-4974-9f5c-9d129eb28387.explicacao: Busking is playing the light live without a fixed cuelist, reacting to the music — common at band shows.

dfc926b6-c2c9-401f-9786-377e5f57d16d.pergunta: What are the four technical pillars of light cited as the lighting professional's 'grammar'?
dfc926b6-c2c9-401f-9786-377e5f57d16d.explicacao: Color temperature, light intensity, distribution, and light pattern are the fundamentals described in the source material.

bc6e211d-2dd7-482e-b335-7beaba6fa06b.pergunta: In a small production, it is common for:
bc6e211d-2dd7-482e-b335-7beaba6fa06b.explicacao: In smaller productions, a single person often designs, rigs, and operates the light.

opcoes.baef0ef4-d197-4828-96c8-c3cd0022dff5: One person to combine LD, operator, and technician
opcoes.e8058b17-d043-4de8-aa7f-c6821c05ea0b: There to be no light operator
opcoes.99d351e9-cec8-436b-b8d6-cd95b814324b: Each role to have 3 dedicated people

3973733e-4e28-47fd-bee7-5c74f545b7f2.pergunta: A 3000 K light is described as:
3973733e-4e28-47fd-bee7-5c74f545b7f2.explicacao: Low temperatures (≈2700–3200 K) produce warm/yellowish white, like incandescent.

b4e4647a-f7ed-4aa8-b593-1b612dbd0aa2.pergunta: If you double the distance between the fixture and the object, the illuminance on the object becomes approximately:
b4e4647a-f7ed-4aa8-b593-1b612dbd0aa2.explicacao: By the inverse-square law, intensity falls off with the square of distance: twice the distance gives about 1/4 of the light.

0d7c8a9c-a210-4e6b-9d16-e0c04c5a08e4.pergunta: To reveal volume and relief on an artist's face, the most recommended angle is usually:
0d7c8a9c-a210-4e6b-9d16-e0c04c5a08e4.explicacao: Side light (classic at 45°) models the face creating relief; front light flattens.

6aa8be39-33bf-411a-81da-05f8c5588220.pergunta: High CRI (>90) is especially important when:
6aa8be39-33bf-411a-81da-05f8c5588220.explicacao: High CRI ensures faithful rendering of skin and costume colors, essential when there is image capture.

e744f3cd-663a-4c24-b9e1-e23ef45b464a.pergunta: A small, hard light source, close to the object, tends to generate:
e744f3cd-663a-4c24-b9e1-e23ef45b464a.explicacao: Small, close sources produce sharp-edged shadows; large/diffuse sources soften the shadow.

7f7745d3-bbaa-4ad9-9de2-24a1fc6eb04a.pergunta: Which NR deals specifically with work at height, mandatory for rigging truss?
7f7745d3-bbaa-4ad9-9de2-24a1fc6eb04a.explicacao: NR 35 regulates work at height (above 2 m), requiring training, a harness, and a lifeline.

5f89a9c6-96f9-43e2-8230-da5f7b9ee64b.pergunta: Every suspended fixture must have, besides the primary clamp:
5f89a9c6-96f9-43e2-8230-da5f7b9ee64b.explicacao: The secondary attachment (steel cable) holds the fixture if the primary clamp fails, preventing a fall.

42e93f4e-5f55-4bf5-9f7f-9de6e17ec342.pergunta: To calculate the current drawn by a circuit, you use:
42e93f4e-5f55-4bf5-9f7f-9de6e17ec342.explicacao: Current = Power ÷ Voltage. Add up the watts and divide by the voltage to find the amperes.

fd3a3a35-085b-4480-b518-976e22652e31.pergunta: The ART (Technical Responsibility Note) at an event is signed by:
fd3a3a35-085b-4480-b518-976e22652e31.explicacao: The ART for electrical installations is the responsibility of an electrical engineer, attesting compliance with the standards.

a016cabd-0af2-4513-9f96-273b99dd94e7.pergunta: Which standard deals with safety in electrical installations and services?
a016cabd-0af2-4513-9f96-273b99dd94e7.explicacao: NR 10 is the central standard for electrical safety, essential for anyone handling AC in the rig.

beb40185-915d-4f89-bd66-7f678ecdc46e.pergunta: How many channels are there in a DMX512 universe?
beb40185-915d-4f89-bd66-7f678ecdc46e.explicacao: Each DMX512 universe has exactly 512 channels, each taking values from 0 to 255.

db47d9fc-c43c-4f0b-9b8f-85eb9efc1b42.pergunta: DMX512 is a protocol that is:
db47d9fc-c43c-4f0b-9b8f-85eb9efc1b42.explicacao: Pure DMX is unidirectional; it is the RDM extension that adds communication back.

opcoes.df0d160d-4ddb-4b51-abfb-ede5a2a710f3: Unidirectional: the console commands and the fixture obeys
opcoes.882d3e03-927e-4d6c-a128-47b3bcf08cee: Bidirectional, with a reply from the fixture

74162638-5e3a-43a5-90d4-4c889f370e33.explicacao: The terminator goes on the DMX OUT of the last piece of equipment in the chain, absorbing signal reflections.

opcoes.4a34b073-ae8d-4ffc-909a-cd375611570f: On the last fixture of the line

5b8bf9f7-f94f-4991-864c-75287e384fa8.pergunta: When the number of channels needed exceeds 512, you should:
5b8bf9f7-f94f-4991-864c-75287e384fa8.explicacao: More than 512 channels requires an additional universe, distributed over another DMX output or node.

91e8b1b2-071a-4372-9380-48904ab12511.pergunta: To transport many universes over a single network cable to the stage, you use:
91e8b1b2-071a-4372-9380-48904ab12511.explicacao: Art-Net and sACN (E1.31) encapsulate DMX in Ethernet; a node converts it back to XLR outputs.

28bc2eb9-86d2-411a-9112-0c1ab0ed1bba.pergunta: An 8-channel fixture is at address 1. The next fixture should start at address:
28bc2eb9-86d2-411a-9112-0c1ab0ed1bba.explicacao: It occupies channels 1 to 8, so the next one starts at 1 + 8 = 9.

cdab574d-944a-4779-884b-02aab99643fe.pergunta: Doing the 'patch' at the console means:
cdab574d-944a-4779-884b-02aab99643fe.explicacao: Patch is telling the console which fixture model/mode is at each address, loading the correct personality.

opcoes.87aaf413-3ffe-477e-bca6-289c0a16baf9: Associating address/universe with a fixture profile (personality)

292bb9f6-6196-4dcd-af35-bbc7dcd48b05.pergunta: What happens if the mode chosen on the fixture is different from the mode in the patch?
292bb9f6-6196-4dcd-af35-bbc7dcd48b05.explicacao: If the modes do not match, the channel mapping is wrong and the parameters respond swapped.

opcoes.0828bd04-b341-48f5-9dc4-f8651896212a: The functions come out swapped (color becomes gobo, tilt becomes dimmer)

1ace2073-f6b5-4b66-a21d-309800752c94.pergunta: For 3 moving heads of 16 channels, the correct addresses without overlap are:
1ace2073-f6b5-4b66-a21d-309800752c94.explicacao: Each one occupies 16 channels: 1–16, 17–32, 33–48, so the start addresses are 1, 17, and 33.

721b92ed-1446-4eb3-af62-e57600111387.pergunta: Older equipment usually has the address set by:
721b92ed-1446-4eb3-af62-e57600111387.explicacao: Legacy fixtures use binary DIP switches, whose combined positions form the address.

ba9a2510-114d-4bee-ac50-6d183afb33c6.pergunta: Which moving head is best for bathing a large area with color smoothly?
ba9a2510-114d-4bee-ac50-6d183afb33c6.explicacao: The Wash has a broad beam and smooth color mixing, ideal for coverage and atmosphere.

2fd18c94-79d4-4c75-90b6-9a1b3c58ff9f.pergunta: The narrow, intense, parallel beam that 'cuts through the air' with smoke is typical of the:
2fd18c94-79d4-4c75-90b6-9a1b3c58ff9f.explicacao: The Beam produces concentrated, parallel rays, the classic aerial festival effect.

16884ba1-20ed-4c92-a98e-937e359af91b.pergunta: To project gobos (patterns and logos) with sharp edges, you use the:
16884ba1-20ed-4c92-a98e-937e359af91b.explicacao: The Spot has gobos, focus, and prism to project defined shapes and textures.

fd380640-f564-4610-8d03-891964dc492b.pergunta: Pan and tilt on a moving head control:
fd380640-f564-4610-8d03-891964dc492b.explicacao: Pan is the horizontal rotation and tilt the vertical movement of the moving head.

opcoes.f8b65f51-4c99-4c54-8ede-5601f1e3719e: The horizontal and vertical movement of the head

5937778d-29eb-408b-8bcd-0f0c04b59f22.pergunta: A hybrid fixture is advantageous mainly because:
5937778d-29eb-408b-8bcd-0f0c04b59f22.explicacao: The hybrid brings together the three functions in one fixture, saving points and weight on the rig.

opcoes.1ef2a79c-464a-451d-91ce-4f64764e4dfe: It combines Spot, Beam, and Wash in a single point of the truss

efd99565-116d-4499-be43-24908a959e42.pergunta: In the additive color mixing of an LED, red + green + blue result in:
efd99565-116d-4499-be43-24908a959e42.explicacao: In additive synthesis, adding the three primaries of light (R+G+B) produces white.

879370a7-23e0-4395-9a2c-8859aced0ebf.pergunta: The fixture designed to light backdrops and cycloramas with uniform color is the:
879370a7-23e0-4395-9a2c-8859aced0ebf.explicacao: The footlight is a wide wash made to bathe vertical surfaces and backdrops without a hard shadow.

12c40854-688e-4039-a082-7f6e1d65e20d.explicacao: PAR comes from 'parabolic aluminized reflector,' the shape of the reflector that directs the light.

3f12cb2f-0b30-4baf-a759-714d574df004.pergunta: LED COB means that the chips are:
3f12cb2f-0b30-4baf-a759-714d574df004.explicacao: COB (Chip-on-Board) groups several chips on one board, generating dense, uniform light.

opcoes.c91daef0-2ce2-4aba-9fcf-8319f8be1f63: Mounted together on a single board (Chip-on-Board)

40ae068d-88c1-46b7-9a69-2f4a7eea60f3.pergunta: A 'brute' fixture used to 'blast' the audience works as a:
40ae068d-88c1-46b7-9a69-2f4a7eea60f3.explicacao: A brute/blinder is a high-power source that dazzles the audience at impact moments.

33a52815-103e-45af-b7d6-2b728ddf9ffd.pergunta: Which effect poses the greatest risk of triggering seizures in photosensitive people?
33a52815-103e-45af-b7d6-2b728ddf9ffd.explicacao: The stroboscopic effect (strobe) can cause discomfort, migraines and seizures in sensitive people.

0065ade0-503e-454f-9cd4-56055759999c.pergunta: To keep the beams visible throughout the entire show, the ideal is to use:
0065ade0-503e-454f-9cd4-56055759999c.explicacao: The hazer creates a fine, homogeneous mist suspended in the air, making the beams continuously visible.

f060887f-4dad-4e11-9693-51cf79e08b34.explicacao: The gobo is a cut-out disc/plate that projects shapes and patterns in the light path.

75804073-c627-4e51-8da3-69d5082b2621.pergunta: Show lasers are rated by hazard and, in higher classes, require:
75804073-c627-4e51-8da3-69d5082b2621.explicacao: Class IIIb/IV lasers require strict precautions: a qualified operator, legal compliance and never aiming at the eyes.

opcoes.2021f159-8aa8-4091-a29a-a223e97cd0b6: Qualified operation and safe sweeping above the audience
opcoes.1e5fd0c6-2a31-47ee-9089-2b795fcaf6d8: Aiming at the eyes to test

55f034df-4a01-4a11-91e6-62ae42f320fa.pergunta: The central difference between a smoke machine and a hazer is that:
55f034df-4a01-4a11-91e6-62ae42f320fa.explicacao: Smoke produces dense, localized fog; the hazer keeps the air slightly diffuse in a continuous, homogeneous way.

opcoes.2fabbfcf-b712-4a1d-acb4-777f246d5a6d: Smoke is electric and the hazer is not
opcoes.ee520ce3-e1ad-4828-9b81-900cb5991fb9: Smoke gives dense/localized fog; the hazer gives fine/continuous mist

39d9c87a-1c6a-4b78-851c-8b1c259eefa4.pergunta: As for power, LED fixtures and moving heads must be connected to:
39d9c87a-1c6a-4b78-851c-8b1c259eefa4.explicacao: LEDs and movings have an internal dimmer and require a constant supply; dimming the power externally damages them.

c81b6ee0-b666-4589-b053-c5c4ba532a65.pergunta: The function of a dimmer is:
c81b6ee0-b666-4589-b053-c5c4ba532a65.explicacao: The dimmer regulates the power delivered to conventional lamps, controlling the intensity.

opcoes.dfe5e582-83f7-42d1-9396-cc60ef775f4b: To control intensity by varying the power of conventional fixtures

288cb542-526f-4aaa-9f13-b9b2ed4900a4.explicacao: The splitter branches DMX into isolated, reinforced lines, avoiding flicker and isolating faults.

opcoes.2e2bce9a-0418-4c63-b767-3bfaead3a3c6: Replicate the DMX signal across several isolated and amplified outputs

2c964f9f-63e8-4176-8fa9-c391c2e48ef7.pergunta: On the power distro, balancing the phases means:
2c964f9f-63e8-4176-8fa9-c391c2e48ef7.explicacao: Balancing phases avoids overload, distributing the fixtures' watts across the available phases.

opcoes.754ad596-ce64-4d71-ac9f-bcd57378cfa5: Using only one phase for everything
opcoes.5699ce13-3937-4bf7-9612-101654b13d35: Distributing the load so that no phase gets overloaded

c296ab04-c8ae-45aa-af76-0f2653340288.pergunta: Good practice when powering down the rig:
c296ab04-c8ae-45aa-af76-0f2653340288.explicacao: Black out before cutting power and turn off in stages to avoid surges and accidents.

opcoes.afd76c52-06a4-4067-a18b-97178fe15f33: Cut the power before the blackout
opcoes.5c4802ee-b6b6-43dc-96af-80c06ce5ff36: Black out on the console and de-energize in stages
opcoes.3374fe0c-f564-497c-9c61-7543e78fe52b: Yank the AC from the hot fixtures immediately

8dd63dd0-75b6-4469-a162-21fa665700b2.pergunta: Which manufacturer is a reference in the theater world, with a 'tracking' cue workflow?
8dd63dd0-75b6-4469-a162-21fa665700b2.explicacao: ETC (Eos, Ion, Element) dominates theater, with very precise, tracking-based cue programming.

70414ca9-5570-48cc-bc87-e4183faf1a58.pergunta: The grandMA2/grandMA3 family is made by:
70414ca9-5570-48cc-bc87-e4183faf1a58.explicacao: grandMA is MA Lighting's line, the standard on major tours, festivals and TV.

72a7e0a9-f6d7-463a-b651-72f430808c8d.pergunta: Which platform is known for offering free software for PC, making learning easier?
72a7e0a9-f6d7-463a-b651-72f430808c8d.explicacao: MagicQ and Onyx offer free versions for PC; grandMA3 also has onPC.

967fb875-14a7-438e-b341-e38af91fff73.pergunta: The operating system of Avolites consoles (Tiger Touch, Titan Mobile) is called:
967fb875-14a7-438e-b341-e38af91fff73.explicacao: Titan is the software that runs on Avolites consoles, nimble for programming and busking.

e3a148bf-5595-4226-8a7e-9519c0276568.pergunta: Before operating an unfamiliar console, the correct attitude is:
e3a148bf-5595-4226-8a7e-9519c0276568.explicacao: Understanding the standard workflow and consulting the specific manual is the safe professional practice.

opcoes.356e9e64-639f-4093-b555-993bc08e8c79: Study the manufacturer's manual and train on the offline software

db929dbe-7de8-4aa8-9611-43f770d725be.pergunta: The universal programming cycle on almost every console is:
db929dbe-7de8-4aa8-9611-43f770d725be.explicacao: Select, adjust and record (Record/Store) is the logic common to practically all consoles.

2357b089-70b2-4cda-8564-a91540049bc8.pergunta: A 'group' on a lighting console is:
2357b089-70b2-4cda-8564-a91540049bc8.explicacao: A group is a set of fixtures saved for quick selection (e.g., all the rear beams).

38e63bdc-c969-4272-a7ec-558866469719.pergunta: The great advantage of palettes being a 'live reference' is that:
38e63bdc-c969-4272-a7ec-558866469719.explicacao: Since scenes point to the palette, adjusting the palette corrects all the scenes at once.

opcoes.977c1217-5f31-4e09-9b1f-2bcd2b34be8a: When you change the palette, all the scenes that use it update together

b6d64fdd-0a84-4fd8-ad73-80df3b8c426a.explicacao: Position palettes store pan/tilt combinations (e.g., lead singer, drums, audience) for reuse.

14a27ff4-3c26-42bf-b3cc-905ee1137ea4.pergunta: The syntax '1 THRU 8 ENTER' normally serves to:
14a27ff4-3c26-42bf-b3cc-905ee1137ea4.explicacao: On the command line, 'THRU' selects a continuous range of fixtures (1 to 8).

152310ab-47ab-4fb0-8d60-f3685d163ad0.pergunta: The ordered sequence of cues run with GO during the show is called:
152310ab-47ab-4fb0-8d60-f3685d163ad0.explicacao: The cuelist is the list of cues run in order; the operator advances with GO.

f7d0e196-8dd4-4b2c-9ef3-6027a237b6c1.pergunta: The 'fade' time of a cue defines:
f7d0e196-8dd4-4b2c-9ef3-6027a237b6c1.explicacao: Fade is the time the light takes to transition from one state to another.

opcoes.2cf54439-9761-4dbe-932c-328d12a3e3c1: The duration of the transition between looks

cf643f4c-0c06-4bc1-8392-4846d645c506.explicacao: In tracking, values propagate through the following cues until they are changed — typical of the theater/ETC workflow.

opcoes.0420cad0-1a12-46c5-a331-bed01f28c8b1: Remains in the following cues until it is changed

26863799-9c1d-470c-8068-bded488be577.explicacao: A chase is an automatic, cyclic sequence, with adjustable speed/BPM.

opcoes.bb698fb3-c3ba-458b-b77a-8a7ee997998b: An automatic rhythmic sequence (e.g., PARs flashing in order)

e2c5b5db-5cdc-49c2-b810-8b3da0704474.pergunta: Why organize the main cuelist on a fader and the effects on separate buttons?
e2c5b5db-5cdc-49c2-b810-8b3da0704474.explicacao: Separating the cuelist from the effects lets the operator follow the show and 'season' it with strobe/blinder/chase live.

opcoes.4be22eda-9eae-4610-adf0-3b17a6bf6d05: To follow the cuelist and trigger effects live smoothly

2e3c8725-aacf-4599-9b3c-0ee10f74cf3c.explicacao: Busking is building looks on the spot, improvising in response to the music, without a fixed cuelist.

opcoes.16ae49cd-f10b-442e-9fef-e609ecebd2c2: Operating the light live, reacting to the music in real time

a0a49de9-073b-47a3-9c24-89e4e948b6bc.explicacao: Tap tempo lets the operator tap the beat and the effect takes that BPM, syncing with the band.

opcoes.d04abd5a-72d5-4480-9e22-8a9a28b3e90a: Match the speed of chases/effects to the music's BPM

f2e9c5d0-4c06-493f-89d0-5d7762bf251b.pergunta: To prepare the console for improvisation, it is essential to have at hand:
f2e9c5d0-4c06-493f-89d0-5d7762bf251b.explicacao: The more ready and labeled it is (groups, palettes, effects), the more the operator reacts to the music.

opcoes.c75f8a4a-c581-4952-94d8-96ca04895575: Groups, palettes, positions and effects on labeled buttons

622f6036-301e-4cc7-a078-b5540747a3ed.pergunta: A good discipline in busking is:
622f6036-301e-4cc7-a078-b5540747a3ed.explicacao: Contrast gives impact: saving strobe/blinder for the climaxes and giving breathing room avoids fatigue and health risk.

opcoes.479f05a5-ea11-4e4e-8074-00d5402ee04d: Keep the strobe and blinder on all the time
opcoes.2ebe33b8-8207-4076-989d-5423d506425b: Reserve the strong effects for the peaks and respect the breathing room

82f8cfcf-3216-478d-8fe7-6fc087e10bf9.explicacao: Thinking in layers (color, position, effect, seasoning) organizes live operation and makes it easier to build and tear down looks.

opcoes.2e4809cd-da7f-44fd-b2ac-9c358eccd4c3: Adding a color base + position + effect + seasoning, and tearing down layers in the breathing-room moments

3d39e5e4-ec6f-40ec-95fe-8b4f411e404a.pergunta: In the example rig (4 PARs 8ch + 2 spots 16ch + 1 hazer 1ch), the total number of channels used is:

20bec623-744c-4cde-bf09-b1490b8c2b0b.pergunta: If PAR4 occupies channels 25–32, the first moving spot (16ch) starts at address:

5fa42047-1a0b-4fde-8d32-6cee9c415ae9.pergunta: Why NOT connect the LED PARs and movings to a dimmer circuit?
5fa42047-1a0b-4fde-8d32-6cee9c415ae9.explicacao: LEDs and movings dim internally via DMX and require a constant supply; dimming the power damages them.

opcoes.6a38a328-d327-4f96-8fef-a0395a9f57cd: Because they have an internal dimmer and need constant power (non-dim)

965f871a-fc52-4c1e-87fa-eeb58e1d8d56.pergunta: During verification, two spots lit up together. The most likely cause is:
965f871a-fc52-4c1e-87fa-eeb58e1d8d56.explicacao: Overlapping addresses make fixtures respond to the same command; you just have to separate the addresses.

opcoes.25974ac7-a341-4f68-b5df-6a05bd9a1182: Both with the same DMX address

c811dcb9-c68d-4b53-a4de-d5fa252a1253.pergunta: The last fixture on the DMX line must receive:
c811dcb9-c68d-4b53-a4de-d5fa252a1253.explicacao: The terminator on the last fixture's DMX OUT stabilizes the signal and avoids flicker.

85d03930-fb79-4fb6-9176-95017b2ebd53.pergunta: Why create 'Even' and 'Odd' groups of the PARs?
85d03930-fb79-4fb6-9176-95017b2ebd53.explicacao: Even/odd groups allow immediate alternations and chases, speeding up operation.

opcoes.8816733f-73d6-4201-972f-505398eaee58: To turn off half the rig
opcoes.577045d4-b22d-45e3-8b8e-bac2fc3ed7de: To make chases and quick alternations easier

11740ed0-7b92-4c97-997a-c450614340c4.pergunta: When creating a position palette, it is advisable to record:
11740ed0-7b92-4c97-997a-c450614340c4.explicacao: Position palettes should contain only pan/tilt so they can be reapplied without dragging along unwanted color/gobo.

opcoes.562e8c28-b48e-4e36-a78f-2726b0187d38: Only pan/tilt, to keep it reusable

66ffb66a-2eb9-4e4d-9760-97afab170342.explicacao: The scene brings together selection (group), color, position, intensity and transition time (fade).

opcoes.a9587601-c9d3-4aee-a0ec-48458a512dbb: Group + color palette + position + intensity + fade

44eef2ca-5604-4bcb-bef1-e58f1b844c98.pergunta: If you readjust the 'Center' palette and the scene that uses it updates by itself, that is:
44eef2ca-5604-4bcb-bef1-e58f1b844c98.explicacao: Since the scene points to the palette, changing the palette propagates the change to the scenes that reference it.

b757afd7-e412-475d-ab7c-f8f6a3f73e44.pergunta: Before testing a newly created scene, it is good practice to:
b757afd7-e412-475d-ab7c-f8f6a3f73e44.explicacao: Clearing the active programming avoids recording 'garbage' and ensures a test faithful to what was saved.

opcoes.074f750c-989f-4b22-a2a0-5ae94ee56a19: Do a 'Clear' on the active programming

f696bb45-3472-4982-9a15-d197f91b74d1.pergunta: When building a cuelist for a song, each cue normally corresponds to:
f696bb45-3472-4982-9a15-d197f91b74d1.explicacao: The musical structure guides the cuelist: each section becomes a cue with its look.

opcoes.bf067597-72e7-4108-bf2d-cca222c3d6ed: Each section of the song (intro, verse, chorus...)

4a056671-5cf5-4172-994e-84e5a611c63e.pergunta: For an explosive chorus, the ideal fade time tends to be:
4a056671-5cf5-4172-994e-84e5a611c63e.explicacao: Fast transitions match the impact of the chorus; long fades serve intros and endings.

55b90857-22eb-4eef-aa58-9c1e224b7e97.pergunta: Combining the cuelist with strobe/blinder on buttons allows:
55b90857-22eb-4eef-aa58-9c1e224b7e97.explicacao: The cuelist gives the skeleton and the effect buttons let you season the accents manually.

opcoes.e2b00be0-e119-4710-8195-291a3acbce14: Having the safe structure of the cuelist and the expressiveness of busking live

576cf26d-5954-4600-9887-2527a403323a.pergunta: Rehearsing the cuelist with the song playing serves mainly to:
576cf26d-5954-4600-9887-2527a403323a.explicacao: Running it with the track lets you fine-tune fades and know the exact point of each GO.
